proposals.title: Charge-Modified Antibody-Liposome Conjugates for Enhanced Delivery of Therapeutic Radionuclides to Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: The efficacy of radioimmunotherapy is often limited by poor tumor penetration and non-specific uptake of radiolabeled antibodies. Pre-2026 work by Torchilin's group and others has demonstrated that modifying the surface charge of nanocarriers like liposomes significantly alters their pharmacokinetics and biodistribution, improving blood pool retention and tissue targeting. Applying this charge-modification principle directly to therapeutic antibody carriers could overcome key delivery barriers in oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: Conjugating charge-modified antibodies (CMAbs) to long-circulating, drug-loaded liposomes will create a dual-targeting platform that synergistically improves the specific delivery and localization of therapeutic radionuclides (e.g., I-131, Y-90) to tumor sites, compared to conventional antibody-drug conjugates or unmodified immunoliposomes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize cationic and anionic variants of a model tumor-targeting antibody (e.g., anti-HER2) via chemical modification of surface lysines with charged moieties. These CMAbs will be conjugated to the surface of PEGylated liposomes pre-loaded with a therapeutic radionuclide chelator (DOTA for Y-90) or a cytotoxic drug (doxorubicin). Using pre-2026 methods, we will characterize physicochemical properties, then evaluate in vitro targeting in relevant cancer cell lines. Pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and therapeutic efficacy will be assessed in vivo using murine xenograft models, with imaging support from gamma-scintigraphy or MRI (using analogous gadolinium-loaded constructs for validation).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a novel therapeutic nanoplatform that merges the specificity of antibodies with the enhanced pharmacokinetics of charge-modified carriers. It will provide critical data on how antibody charge influences the delivery profile of liposomal payloads, potentially leading to more effective and safer radioimmunotherapeutics with reduced off-target toxicity.
proposals.novelty: While charge-modified liposomes and immunoliposomes have been explored separately, the direct application of charge modification to the antibody component of an antibody-liposome conjugate (rather than the liposome lipid bilayer) to actively steer the biodistribution of a therapeutic radionuclide carrier is a novel and unexplored strategy.

proposals.title: Charge-Modified Full-Length IgG Antibodies: A Novel Strategy to Control Biodistribution and Blood-Pool Retention for Improved Imaging and Delivery
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, controlling the pharmacokinetics and biodistribution of monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) remains a significant challenge. While PEGylation is common, it primarily affects size and can induce immunogenicity. Torchilin's lab has pioneered the use of synthetic charged polymers (e.g., polylysine derivatives) to modify liposomes and smaller proteins, dramatically altering their blood clearance and tissue uptake, but this approach has not been systematically applied to intact, full-length IgG antibodies, which are critical therapeutic and diagnostic agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that covalent modification of intact IgG antibodies with defined synthetic polycationic or polyanionic polymers will significantly alter their charge-dependent interactions with biological surfaces, leading to predictable and tunable changes in blood-pool retention, tissue biodistribution, and cellular uptake kinetics without compromising antigen-binding affinity.
proposals.method: We will conjugate poly-L-lysine (PLL) or polyglutamic acid (PGA) chains of defined length (5-20 kDa) to surface lysines of model IgGs (e.g., trastuzumab, rituximab) using NHS chemistry, controlling the degree of substitution. Charge, size, and aggregation will be characterized by zeta potential, DLS, and SEC. Antigen binding will be verified by ELISA and surface plasmon resonance. Biodistribution and blood-pool kinetics will be quantified in vivo using radioiodination (I-125) or by conjugating the antibodies with gadolinium-loaded liposomes for dynamic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), comparing modified vs. native IgG in rodent models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, charge-based engineering platform for full-length antibodies, expanding the toolbox beyond PEGylation. It will provide fundamental data on how macromolecular charge dictates antibody pharmacokinetics, potentially leading to longer-circulating imaging agents for vascular targets or, conversely, faster-clearing agents for reduced off-target effects. It directly extends Torchilin's expertise in charge-modified carriers to a major biomolecular class.
proposals.novelty: While charge modification is established for liposomes and peptides, its application to intact, therapeutic IgG antibodies to systematically steer their in vivo fate is unexplored. This merges the field of antibody engineering with a proven, non-PEG polymer modification strategy from the drug delivery community.

proposals.title: Charge Heterogeneity Analysis of TAT-Peptide and Antibody-Conjugated Liposomes by High-Resolution Capillary Zone Electrophoresis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, liposomal nanocarriers functionalized with targeting ligands like antibodies or cell-penetrating peptides (e.g., TAT) are critical for drug delivery and imaging. However, their surface charge (zeta potential) is a key determinant of stability, circulation time, and cellular interaction, yet standard dynamic light scattering provides only an average measurement, masking critical heterogeneity introduced by conjugation chemistry. A detailed understanding of this charge distribution is essential for rational design and batch consistency of complex nanomedicines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-resolution capillary zone electrophoresis (CZE) will resolve sub-populations within batches of antibody- or TAT-conjugated liposomes based on their surface charge, revealing heterogeneity that correlates with functional performance in vitro.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes loaded with a gadolinium-based MRI contrast agent, following Torchilin's established long-circulating liposome (LCL) protocol. Conjugation with model antibodies or TAT-peptide will be performed. Charge characterization will employ a Beckman PA 800 Plus capillary electrophoresis system with a fused-silica capillary and UV detection. We will optimize the background electrolyte (e.g., borate or phosphate buffers at physiological pH) and applied voltage to achieve separation of liposome populations. Data will be analyzed using 32 Karat Software to deconvolute electrophoregram peaks, quantifying the proportion of liposomes in distinct charge states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish CZE as a robust, high-resolution analytical tool for the nanomedicine field, moving beyond average zeta potential. It will provide a new quality control metric for surface-modified liposomes and yield fundamental insights into how conjugation efficiency and chemistry impact the charge distribution of complex nanocarriers.
proposals.novelty: While CZE is used for protein analysis, its application to resolve charge heterogeneity of intact, functionalized liposomal nanocarriers—bridging Torchilin's expertise in liposome engineering with advanced analytical separation science—is novel and addresses a critical, unmet characterization need.

proposals.title: Multivalent Polymeric Nanoparticles for Enhanced Blood-Pool Imaging and Targeted Contrast Delivery
proposals.motivation: Current long-circulating liposomal contrast agents (e.g., gadolinium-loaded) provide excellent blood-pool imaging but lack robust, amplified targeting to specific vascular epitopes. For diagnostic imaging and theranostics, there is a need for carriers with higher ligand density and avidity than liposomes can provide, to improve detection sensitivity for low-abundance biomarkers in cardiovascular disease and oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large, long-circulating polymeric nanoparticle core, densely functionalized with charge-modified antibodies, will provide superior multivalent binding avidity and signal amplification for molecular MRI compared to antibody-conjugated liposomes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize 80-120 nm poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles using a nanoprecipitation method, incorporating gadolinium-DTPA complexes and PEG-lipid conjugates for stealth properties. Surface amine groups will be activated for covalent conjugation of antibodies (e.g., anti-ICAM-1) pre-modified with cationic charges to enhance electrostatic interaction with the anionic nanoparticle surface, maximizing loading density. Binding avidity and specificity will be tested in vitro using activated endothelial cells under flow, and in vivo pharmacokinetics and targeting efficacy will be evaluated in a murine inflammation model using a 7T MRI scanner.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable platform for creating high-avidity, antibody-decorated polymeric nanocarriers for imaging. It will directly compare the targeting performance of this platform against the established TAT- and gadolinium-loaded liposome technologies from the Torchilin lab, providing a clear advancement in carrier design for molecular diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: While antibody conjugation to nanoparticles is known, the strategic use of charge-modified antibodies to achieve ultra-high, oriented surface density on a single polymeric core for combined blood-pool persistence and amplified targeting is a novel synthesis of pre-2026 concepts in nanocarrier engineering and molecular imaging.

proposals.title: A Highly Charged Liposomal Clearing Agent for the Rapid Extracorporeal Removal of Non-Targeted Monoclonal Antibodies from Blood
proposals.motivation: Monoclonal antibody (mAb) therapies can cause severe off-target toxicity due to non-specific binding. Current methods to mitigate this, like dose fractionation or anti-idiotypic antibodies, are slow, expensive, or impractical for rapid intervention. A universal method to quickly clear unbound, circulating mAb from the bloodstream after administration would enhance therapeutic safety and enable higher, more effective doses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a long-circulating, highly charged liposome will act as an effective non-specific scavenger, binding and sequestering circulating mAbs via electrostatic interactions, enabling their rapid removal via extracorporeal blood filtration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PEGylated liposomes with a high density of surface charge using cationic (e.g., DOTAP) or anionic (e.g., DOPG) lipids. Liposome charge, size, and PEG density will be optimized for maximal mAb binding in serum, measured by ELISA and surface plasmon resonance. The optimal 'clearing liposome' will be tested in vitro in human plasma spiked with fluorescently labeled IgG1 (rituximab). Its efficacy will be evaluated in vivo in a rodent model by administering the mAb, followed by the clearing agent, and then monitoring mAb blood concentration over time via fluorescence or radioisotope tracing, with and without a single pass through a model extracorporeal filter.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for a universal, charge-based pharmacologic clearing system. It will provide a novel tool to manage mAb toxicity and improve therapeutic windows. The platform could be extended to clear other positively or negatively charged pathogenic proteins from circulation.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Torchilin's lab pioneered long-circulating liposomes for drug and contrast agent delivery, this proposal inverts the paradigm, using charge not for targeting delivery to cells, but for systemic sequestration and removal. The concept of a non-specific, charge-based scavenger liposome for extracorporeal clearance is distinct from existing antigen-specific clearing approaches.

proposals.title: Active Tumor Targeting of Long-Circulating Iodinated Polymeric Micelles for Enhanced Computed Tomography Angiography and Imaging
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, long-circulating 'blood pool' contrast agents, such as liposomal or micellar iodine carriers, rely on passive accumulation via the Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect, which is variable and inefficient. For computed tomography (CT), which requires high local concentrations of iodine, active targeting could significantly improve contrast-to-noise ratios in specific vascular beds or at disease sites like tumor neovasculature, enabling more sensitive and specific diagnostic imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: Functionalizing long-circulating, iodine-loaded polymeric micelles with a targeting ligand (e.g., RGD peptide) will confer active, specific binding to αvβ3 integrins expressed on tumor endothelial cells, leading to enhanced and sustained accumulation at the target site compared to non-targeted micelles, thereby improving CT imaging efficacy.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PEG-PE-based polymeric micelles loaded with a lipophilic iodine contrast agent (e.g., iohexol derivative). The micelle surface will be functionalized with cyclic RGD peptides via a PEG-spacer using a maleimide-thiol coupling reaction. In vitro binding will be validated using αvβ3 integrin-expressing endothelial cells. In vivo efficacy will be assessed in a murine tumor model (e.g., Lewis lung carcinoma) using dynamic CT imaging to compare the pharmacokinetics, blood pool retention, and specific tumor vascular accumulation of targeted versus non-targeted iodinated micelles.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel actively targeted nanoparticulate CT contrast agent, moving beyond passive EPR reliance. It will provide a methodology for enhancing vascular contrast in specific pathological tissues, potentially improving early tumor detection and characterization. The platform could be adapted for theranostic applications by co-loading drugs.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's group has pioneered long-circulating liposomal agents for MRI (gadolinium) and passive CT, the active targeting of iodine-loaded micelles specifically for enhanced CT angiography of tumor vasculature is unexplored. This combines the group's expertise in long-circulating nanocarriers and contrast agents with a shift to active, ligand-driven targeting for a modality requiring high payload delivery.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Nanocarrier Platforms for Myocardial Protection: Evaluating ATP-Loaded Liposomes, Polymeric Micelles, and Chemically Modified ATP Analogues in a Rat Heart Model of Global Ischemia
proposals.motivation: Liposomal encapsulation is the established gold standard for delivering labile therapeutics like ATP to ischemic myocardium, as demonstrated by Torchilin's prior work. However, the necessity of the liposome platform itself has not been rigorously challenged. Determining if simpler or more stable nanocarriers can provide equal cardioprotection would streamline therapeutic development and potentially improve clinical translation by addressing liposome-specific limitations like scale-up cost and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alternative nanocarrier systems, specifically ATP-loaded polymeric micelles and membrane-permeant, chemically modified ATP prodrugs, can provide equivalent or superior protection of myocardial mechanical function during ischemia-reperfusion injury compared to traditional ATP-loaded liposomes.
proposals.method: Using the established isolated rat heart (Langendorff) model of global ischemia, we will directly compare three experimental ATP-delivery strategies against the prior liposomal ATP standard and controls. First, ATP will be encapsulated in long-circulating PEG-PE polymeric micelles, a core Torchilin lab technology. Second, we will synthesize and test a panel of ATP prodrugs modified with lipophilic moieties (e.g., POM, SATE groups) to enhance cellular uptake. Functional outcomes (left ventricular developed pressure, +dP/dt) will be the primary metrics. A standardized protocol of 20-min ischemia followed by 40-min reperfusion with pre-ischemic infusion of agents will be used for all groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish whether the cardioprotective effect is contingent on the liposomal carrier or can be achieved via other nanomedicine principles. It will provide a direct, functional comparison of major nanocarrier classes (vesicular vs. micellar) and a prodrug strategy for nucleotide delivery, informing future design of cardiac rescue therapies. The results could shift the paradigm from a focus on liposomes to a focus on the critical delivery parameters for ATP.
proposals.novelty: While liposomal ATP delivery is proven, a head-to-head functional evaluation against other nanocarriers and prodrug forms of ATP in a rigorous ischemia model has not been performed. The question of 'platform necessity' is novel and addresses a fundamental assumption in the field of therapeutic nucleotide delivery.

proposals.title: Comparative Evaluation of Fab' and scFv Fragments for Targeting Long-Circulating Iodinated Liposomes to the Myocardial Blood Pool
proposals.motivation: Targeted long-circulating liposomes, such as those loaded with iodine for CT imaging or ATP for therapy, require efficient and specific homing ligands. Whole monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) can confer immunogenicity, poor tissue penetration, and unfavorable pharmacokinetics, limiting their utility in cardiovascular targeting. Replacing whole mAbs with smaller antibody fragments (e.g., Fab', scFv) may overcome these limitations while retaining specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating Fab' or single-chain variable fragment (scFv) antibodies, specific to a myocardial endothelial marker (e.g., PECAM-1 or ICAM-1), to PEGylated iodinated liposomes will yield superior pharmacokinetics, enhanced target site penetration, and more efficient blood pool contrast or drug delivery compared to whole IgG-conjugated counterparts.
proposals.method: We will prepare long-circulating PEG-liposomes loaded with a non-ionic iodinated contrast agent (e.g., iohexol). Anti-PECAM-1 IgG, Fab' (generated via pepsin digestion and reduction), and scFv (produced recombinantly) will be conjugated to distal PEG termini using maleimide-thiol chemistry. Liposomes will be characterized for size, iodine load, and conjugation efficiency. Pharmacokinetics and biodistribution will be evaluated in a rat model of global myocardial ischemia, comparing blood clearance and myocardial accumulation using gamma-scintigraphy (if radiolabeled) or CT imaging. Intracellular delivery potential will be assessed in vitro using TAT-liposome methodology as a positive control for penetration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, systematic comparison of antibody fragment performance for targeting a clinically relevant cardiovascular compartment. It will establish design principles for next-generation targeted liposomal agents for diagnostic imaging (blood pool CT/MRI) or therapy (e.g., ATP delivery) of ischemic heart disease. The findings will clarify the trade-offs between target affinity, pharmacokinetics, and tissue penetration for different ligand formats.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's lab has pioneered long-circulating contrast agents and intracellular carriers (TAT-liposomes), a direct head-to-head comparison of Fab' vs. scFv for targeting the myocardial vasculature with iodinated liposomes is unexplored. This integrates the lab's expertise in blood pool agents, ischemic heart models, and ligand conjugation into a novel, rational design study.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of Off-Target Accumulation and Clearance Pathways of PEGylated Immunoliposomes in Liver, Spleen, and Kidneys
proposals.motivation: PEGylated immunoliposomes are promising targeted drug delivery systems, but their long-term fate and safety profile remain incompletely understood. While their extended circulation is well-documented, systematic quantification of their off-target biodistribution in major clearance organs (liver, spleen, kidneys) is lacking, which is critical for predicting potential toxicity and optimizing therapeutic indices for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that PEG-immunoliposomes, despite their targeting ligands and stealth properties, will exhibit a quantifiable and ligand-dependent off-target accumulation profile in the reticuloendothelial system (RES) and kidneys, and that this accumulation correlates with specific clearance pathways distinct from non-targeted long-circulating liposomes.
proposals.method: We will prepare a series of liposomes: (1) non-targeted PEG-liposomes, (2) PEG-immunoliposomes conjugated with anti-EGFR F(ab')2 fragments (a well-established model), and (3) control IgG-conjugated liposomes. All will be co-loaded with a gamma-emitting radioisotope (e.g., 111In via the DTPA method) for precise quantitative biodistribution and a near-infrared fluorescent dye (e.g., DiR) for complementary optical imaging. Biodistribution will be quantified at multiple time points (1, 4, 24, 48 h) post-injection in tumor-bearing mice using gamma counting of harvested organs and ex vivo fluorescence imaging. Histological analysis (fluorescence microscopy of tissue sections) will localize liposomes within organ substructures (e.g., liver Kupffer cells, spleen macrophages, renal glomeruli/tubules).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative dataset on the organ- and cell-specific off-target accumulation of a model PEG-immunoliposome. It will delineate the relative contributions of the RES and renal systems to their clearance, directly informing safety assessments. The results will establish a methodological framework for profiling the biodistribution of future targeted nanocarriers.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's prior work quantified blood pool retention (e.g., Gd-loaded liposomes for MRI), this proposal systematically quantifies and compares the off-target organ deposition of immunoliposomes versus standard stealth liposomes, linking distribution data to specific histological structures to elucidate clearance mechanisms.

proposals.title: Humanized Anti-Myosin Immunoliposomes for Reduced Immunogenicity and Enhanced Targeted Delivery in Myocardial Injury Imaging
proposals.motivation: Antimyosin antibody-conjugated liposomes are promising for targeted imaging of myocardial necrosis. However, the murine origin of the antibody (e.g., R11D10) can induce human anti-mouse antibody (HAMA) responses, limiting repeated administration and clinical translation. This immunogenicity problem mirrors challenges overcome in cancer therapy through antibody humanization.
proposals.hypothesis: That replacing the murine antimyosin antibody with a humanized or chimeric version will significantly reduce the immunogenicity of antimyosin immunoliposomes while preserving their specific binding affinity to intracellular myosin exposed in necrotic cardiomyocytes.
proposals.method: We will engineer a humanized antimyosin Fab' fragment using CDR-grafting onto a human IgG framework, based on pre-2026 protocols. This fragment will be conjugated to the surface of long-circulating PEGylated liposomes (similar to our gadolinium-loaded blood-pool agents) via a maleimide-thiol linkage from the Fab' hinge. Liposomes will be co-loaded with a gamma-emitting radioisotope of iodine (I-123) for SPECT imaging. Binding affinity and specificity will be validated in vitro using hypoxic/necrotic cardiomyocytes and compared to murine immunoliposomes. Immunogenicity will be assessed by measuring anti-liposome antibody titers in mice after repeated injection.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a next-generation, clinically translatable targeted imaging agent for acute myocardial infarction. It will establish a methodology for applying antibody humanization techniques from oncology to the field of cardiovascular nanomedicine, directly addressing a key barrier to theranostic agent development.
proposals.novelty: While antibody humanization is standard in cancer, its application to modify immunoliposomes for cardiac molecular imaging is novel. This approach specifically tackles the immunogenicity of antimyosin-targeted carriers, a recognized but unaddressed problem in pre-2026 cardiology nanomedicine.

proposals.title: Targeted Immunomodulation of Myocardial Infarction Using Long-Circulating Liposomes Co-Loaded with Dexamethasone and a Myocardial Homing Peptide
proposals.motivation: Following myocardial infarction (MI), excessive and prolonged inflammation driven by infiltrating immune cells contributes to adverse remodeling and heart failure. While systemic anti-inflammatory therapy is limited by off-target effects, there is a critical need for targeted delivery to the infarct zone. Liposomes, proven as effective carriers for cardioprotective agents (e.g., ATP) and long-circulating blood-pool contrast agents, offer a promising platform to address this unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that long-circulating liposomes decorated with a heart-targeting peptide (e.g., atrial natriuretic peptide fragment) and loaded with the potent glucocorticoid dexamethasone phosphate will selectively accumulate in the infarcted myocardium and attenuate detrimental post-infarct inflammation, thereby preserving viable tissue and cardiac function.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes using a lipid film hydration-extrusion method, co-encapsulating dexamethasone phosphate and a gadolinium-based MRI contrast agent (Gd-DTPA-BMA) for tracking. Liposomes will be surface-functionalized with a cysteine-terminated atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) derivative via a maleimide-PEG-DSPE linker. In a rat model of permanent coronary artery ligation, we will administer targeted vs. non-targeted liposomes intravenously 2 hours post-MI. Biodistribution will be assessed in vivo by T1-weighted MRI (exploiting the Gd payload) and ex vivo by gamma-counting of a co-encapsulated radiotracer. Cardiac function (echocardiography), infarct size (TTC staining), and inflammatory cell infiltration (histology for neutrophils and macrophages) will be evaluated at 3 and 7 days post-MI.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel theranostic liposomal platform that merges diagnostic imaging capability with targeted immunomodulation for post-MI therapy. It will provide critical proof-of-concept data on the feasibility of redirecting a blood-pool imaging vehicle to a therapeutic, cardioprotective modality, potentially defining a new treatment paradigm for acute coronary syndromes.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the established application of long-circulating Gd-liposomes from passive blood-pool imaging agents to active, targeted drug delivery systems for a specific pathological site. The combination of a validated cardiac homing peptide with an anti-inflammatory payload in a single, imageable liposome construct is a novel strategy not yet reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Role of Cholesterol in the Targetability and Pharmacokinetics of Ligand-Modified Long-Circulating Liposomes
proposals.motivation: Cholesterol is a ubiquitous component of long-circulating (e.g., PEGylated) liposomes, primarily to enhance membrane stability and reduce clearance. However, its influence on the function of surface-conjugated targeting ligands (e.g., antibodies, peptides) is not fully decoupled from its pharmacokinetic effects. Given the drive for simpler, more defined nanocarriers, it is critical to test if cholesterol is strictly necessary for achieving both targetability and prolonged circulation, a cornerstone of Torchilin's lab work on targeted imaging and drug delivery agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for PEGylated liposomes decorated with a targeting moiety, the positive correlation between specific targetability and reduced non-specific clearance can be maintained even in cholesterol-free formulations, provided alternative membrane-stabilizing strategies are employed.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes loaded with a gadolinium-based MRI contrast agent, with and without cholesterol (30 mol%), and functionalize them with the TAT peptide (a lab hallmark for intracellular delivery) or a model antibody fragment. Liposome stability will be assessed by monitoring leakage and size. Pharmacokinetics and biodistribution will be quantified in vivo in rats using gamma-scintigraphy (with a co-loaded lipid radiotracer like In-111) and MRI blood pool imaging. Targetability will be evaluated ex vivo in cell cultures and in vivo in a model with accessible target cells (e.g., inflamed endothelium).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly challenge a paradigm in liposome design, potentially simplifying formulation. It will provide a clear, mechanistic dataset separating the roles of cholesterol in stability, stealth, and ligand presentation. The findings could inform the design of next-generation targeted contrast agents and drug carriers with fewer components.
proposals.novelty: While cholesterol-free liposomes have been studied for basic stability, their performance in the context of active targeting and long-circulating stealth properties remains unexplored. This systematically tests the assumed interdependence of cholesterol, circulation time, and targeting efficiency.

proposals.title: Engineering PEGylated Liposomes with Cleavable and Branched Architectures for Enhanced Target Cell Binding and Intracellular Delivery
proposals.motivation: PEGylation is essential for creating long-circulating liposomes, as demonstrated by Torchilin's work on blood pool imaging agents. However, the 'PEG dilemma' persists: the polymer corona sterically hinders the binding of surface-conjugated targeting ligands (e.g., antibodies, TAT peptides) to their cellular receptors, limiting the efficacy of targeted intracellular delivery. Overcoming this barrier is critical for advancing theranostic nanocarriers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically designing the PEG architecture and its conjugation chemistry—specifically using pH-sensitive cleavable linkers and branched, lower-molecular-weight PEG chains—we can preserve long circulation while enabling efficient, condition-specific exposure of targeting motifs to achieve enhanced cell binding and internalization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize liposomes co-modified with two PEG types: (1) traditional long-chain PEG-DSPE for stealth, and (2) a novel component where anti-ICAM-1 antibodies or TAT peptides are attached via a hydrazone-based pH-cleavable linker to shorter, branched PEG-DSPE (e.g., 2kDa PEG). Binding kinetics and internalization will be quantified in vitro using ICAM-1-expressing endothelial cells via flow cytometry and confocal microscopy. In vivo performance (circulation half-life and target site accumulation) will be evaluated in a murine inflammation model using liposomes loaded with a gadolinium-based MRI contrast agent, building on Torchilin's prior blood pool imaging methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a design blueprint for 'smart' PEGylated liposomes that dynamically shed their steric shield at the target site. It will directly contribute to the field of targeted drug delivery and molecular imaging by resolving a key limitation of current stealth nanocarriers, potentially improving the efficacy of therapies for conditions like ischemia or cancer.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard assumption that PEG is solely a hindrance by systematically engineering it as a programmable, conditionally removable component. Combining cleavable linkers with branched PEG architectures for targeted ligand presentation is an underexplored strategy to decouple longevity from binding.

proposals.title: Evaluation of Poly(2-oxazoline)-Phosphatidylethanolamine and Poly(vinylpyrrolidone)-Lipid Conjugates as Novel Steric Barriers for Long-Circulating Immunoliposomes
proposals.motivation: Polyethylene glycol (PEG)-phospholipid conjugates are the gold standard for creating long-circulating (stealth) liposomes, but pre-2026 evidence suggests PEG can induce immune responses (anti-PEG antibodies) and may sterically hinder the binding of targeting ligands (e.g., antibodies, TAT peptides) on immunoliposome surfaces. GM1 ganglioside is an alternative but is complex and costly. There is a clear need for new, effective steric barrier lipids that prolong circulation without compromising subsequent target cell binding, a critical requirement for efficient drug and contrast agent delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conjugating phosphatidylethanolamine (PE) with hydrophilic polymers alternative to PEG, specifically poly(2-oxazoline) (POx) and poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP), will create liposomes with comparable or superior pharmacokinetic profiles to PEG-PE liposomes while presenting a less inhibitory surface for the functional attachment and activity of targeting ligands.
proposals.method: We will synthesize POx-PE and PVP-PE conjugates and incorporate them into PE-rich liposome formulations. Liposome size, zeta potential, and stability will be characterized. Circulation half-life will be evaluated in vivo in mice using liposomes loaded with a gamma-emitting radionuclide (e.g., In-111) or a near-infrared dye, comparing directly to GM1- and PEG-PE-containing controls. The optimized long-circulating formulations will then be functionalized with model targeting ligands (e.g., anti-ICAM-1 antibody or TAT peptide). The binding efficiency and cellular uptake of these immunoliposomes will be quantitatively assessed in vitro using relevant cell lines and compared to their PEGylated counterparts.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically compare the pharmacokinetic and targeting performance of two promising alternative polymer-lipid conjugates against established standards. It will provide a concrete structure-activity relationship for non-PEG steric barriers in the context of immunoliposomes, informing future design of targeted delivery systems for drugs and diagnostic agents with potentially reduced immunogenicity and improved binding kinetics.
proposals.novelty: While POx and PVP have been explored as biomaterials, their direct comparison as PE-conjugated steric barriers for liposomes, with a specific focus on resolving the conflict between longevity and targetability in immunoliposomes, is novel. This moves beyond simply testing circulation time to explicitly evaluating the downstream functional consequence for active targeting.

proposals.title: Quantitative Analysis of PEG-Induced Binding Hindrance on Liposomal Surfaces Using Surface Plasmon Resonance
proposals.motivation: Polyethylene glycol (PEG) coating is a cornerstone strategy for creating long-circulating liposomes, as demonstrated in Dr. Torchilin's work on blood-pool imaging agents. However, its 'stealth' effect, which reduces opsonization, can also inadvertently hinder the binding of targeting ligands like antibodies to their antigens, compromising active targeting efficiency. A quantitative, kinetic understanding of this steric hindrance is critically lacking but is essential for rationally designing next-generation targeted nanocarriers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the density and molecular weight of PEG-lipid conjugates on a liposome surface will quantitatively reduce the apparent association rate (ka) and maximum binding capacity (Rmax) of a model antibody-antigen interaction, as measured by Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR), with the effect following a predictable, non-linear relationship.
proposals.method: We will fabricate liposomes with surface-immobilized antigens (e.g., biotin) and varying densities of DSPE-PEG(2000 or 5000). Using a Biacore SPR system, these liposomes will be captured on a streptavidin sensor chip. The binding kinetics of a monoclonal antibody (IgG) in solution will be measured in real-time. Sensorgrams will be fitted using the Langmuir 1:1 binding model and a two-state reaction model (BIAevaluation software) to extract ka, kd, and Rmax. Control experiments will use non-PEGylated liposomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative dataset correlating PEGylation parameters with the kinetic impairment of antibody binding on a clinically relevant liposome platform. It will establish SPR as a key tool for pre-screening stealth/targeting trade-offs in nanocarrier design, directly informing the development of more effective targeted therapies and imaging agents.
proposals.novelty: While the stealth effect of PEG is well-known qualitatively, applying SPR to directly and kinetically quantify the 'binding hindrance' on intact, PEGylated liposomes—as opposed to flat model surfaces—is novel and provides a direct, physiologically relevant measurement for nanomedicine optimization.

proposals.title: Overcoming the PEG Dilemma: High-Density Antibody Conjugation on PEGylated Liposomes for Enhanced Active Targeting
proposals.motivation: PEGylation is essential for creating long-circulating liposomal drug carriers, but the polymer brush creates a steric barrier that significantly shields conjugated targeting ligands (e.g., antibodies), diminishing cell-specific binding. This 'PEG dilemma' limits the efficacy of actively targeted nanomedicines. Pre-2026 work, including Torchilin's on TAT-liposomes and long-circulating blood-pool agents, highlights the need to reconcile longevity with effective targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dramatically increasing the surface density of conjugated antibodies on PEGylated liposomes will create multivalent binding sites that can project through or locally displace the PEG corona, thereby restoring high-affinity target recognition and cellular uptake.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes using DSPC/Cholesterol with 5 mol% DSPE-PEG(2000)-Maleimide. Using a post-insertion technique for control, we will systematically conjugate a model antibody (e.g., anti-HER2) at increasing densities (from ~10 to >200 antibodies/liposome) via thiol-maleimide chemistry. Liposomes will be characterized for size, zeta potential, and conjugation efficiency. In vitro binding affinity (SPR), avidity (flow cytometry), and internalization (confocal microscopy) will be quantified using HER2-overexpressing SK-BR-3 cells versus control cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative relationship between antibody density and target binding efficiency on stealth liposomes, providing a practical design principle to bypass PEG shielding. It will directly inform the development of next-generation targeted liposomal therapeutics and diagnostics with optimized pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.
proposals.novelty: While ligand conjugation to PEG termini is standard, the systematic exploration of ultra-high ligand density as a direct strategy to overcome PEG-induced steric inhibition is novel and counterintuitive, moving beyond simply optimizing PEG length or using cleavable linkages.

proposals.title: Systematic Design of RES-Avid Liposomes for Enhanced Hepato-Splenic Delivery: An Inverse Engineering Approach
proposals.motivation: For decades, liposome research has focused on evading the reticuloendothelial system (RES) to achieve long circulation for tumor targeting. However, efficient delivery to RES organs like the liver and spleen is critical for treating infectious diseases, lysosomal storage disorders, and primary liver cancers. A systematic, inverse design strategy to deliberately maximize RES uptake remains underexplored, relying instead on empirical observations from failed stealth formulations.
proposals.hypothesis: By systematically inverting the physicochemical principles used to create long-circulating 'stealth' liposomes, we can rationally design liposome formulations with predictably enhanced and tunable affinity for RES macrophages in the liver and spleen.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a combinatorial library of liposomes varying key parameters known to influence RES clearance: size (150-400 nm), surface charge (highly positive or negative zeta potential), and lipid composition (high cholesterol content, inclusion of phosphatidylserine). These will be benchmarked against PEGylated stealth liposomes. Using an isolated perfused rat liver model and in vivo biodistribution studies in mice with radiolabeled (e.g., I-125) liposomes, we will quantify hepatic and splenic uptake. The optimal RES-avid formulation will be loaded with a model drug (e.g., gadolinium for MRI contrast) to demonstrate functional delivery via magnetic resonance imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a rational design framework for RES-targeted liposomal carriers, moving beyond empirical approaches. It will provide a validated set of formulation guidelines for maximizing hepatic and splenic delivery, directly applicable to developing therapies for RES-localized diseases. The project will also yield a novel, highly efficient blood-pool clearance agent for potential diagnostic imaging of liver architecture and function.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant paradigm of liposome design from 'avoidance' to 'active recruitment' of the RES, applying known principles in a novel, systematic way to solve a distinct therapeutic need. It leverages Prof. Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating and intracellular carriers to engineer their conceptual opposite.

proposals.title: Enhancing Tumor Delivery and Cellular Uptake via pH-Sensitive, TATp-Modified Liposomes with Sheddable PEG for Sequential Targeting
proposals.motivation: Liposomal drug delivery to tumors is hindered by the need for both long circulation (via PEGylation) to reach the target and subsequent efficient cellular internalization, which PEG sterically inhibits. Pre-2026, stimuli-responsive liposomes that shed PEG in the tumor microenvironment offer a promising solution, but their combination with a potent secondary cell-penetrating peptide like TATp for sequential targeting is underexplored. This work directly builds on Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating liposomes and TATp-mediated intracellular delivery to address this critical bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: Liposomes engineered with a pH-sensitive PEG-lipid conjugate and a TATp peptide will maintain prolonged blood circulation, then selectively shed their PEG coat in the acidic tumor interstitium to expose TATp, thereby achieving a sequential two-step targeting strategy that enhances both tumor accumulation and cancer cell internalization.
proposals.method: We will synthesize liposomes using a pH-sensitive hydrazone-based PEG-DSPE conjugate and incorporate a TATp-lipid conjugate. Liposome stability and PEG shedding kinetics will be characterized in buffers mimicking physiological (pH 7.4) and tumor (pH 6.5-6.8) pH. In vitro, cellular uptake in cancer cells will be quantified via flow cytometry using fluorescently labeled liposomes. In vivo efficacy will be evaluated in murine tumor models using gadolinium-loaded versions for MRI-based blood pool and tumor accumulation imaging, followed by biodistribution studies of therapeutic (e.g., doxorubicin) payloads.
proposals.contribution: This project will provide a novel, rationally designed nanocarrier platform that decouples the conflicting requirements of circulation and cellular uptake. It will deliver critical proof-of-concept data on the sequential targeting paradigm, potentially improving the therapeutic index of encapsulated drugs. The work will also advance the application of MRI-monitored liposomes, extending Torchilin's prior work on gadolinium-loaded blood-pool agents to dynamic tumor targeting.
proposals.novelty: While pH-sensitive liposomes and TATp-liposomes exist, their integration into a single system where the exposure of TATp is *controlled* and *triggered* by the tumor microenvironment's pH after initial PEG-mediated circulation represents a non-obvious, biomimetic two-stage targeting strategy not fully realized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Ligand-Directed Targeting of Long-Circulating Liposomes to Leukocytes for Inflammatory Blood Pool Imaging
proposals.motivation: Targeted imaging of vascular inflammation remains a challenge. While endothelial-targeted agents exist, they often have limited accessibility to inflamed sites. Circulating leukocytes, which naturally bind to activated endothelium via specific ligands, present a dynamic, self-delivering cellular target. Exploiting this could provide a novel strategy for sensitive, early detection of inflammatory conditions like atherosclerosis.
proposals.hypothesis: Liposomes decorated with endothelial adhesion ligands (e.g., E-selectin or VCAM-1 mimetics) will bind selectively to circulating leukocytes expressing the cognate receptors, creating a leukocyte-hitchhiked, long-circulating contrast agent for enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the inflammatory blood pool.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated, gadolinium-loaded liposomes (following Torchilin's long-circulating liposome methodology) and conjugate them with cyclic peptides mimicking E-selectin or VCAM-1 binding motifs. In vitro, flow cytometry and confocal microscopy will assess ligand-specific binding to activated human leukocytes (e.g., THP-1 monocytes). In vivo efficacy will be tested in an ApoE-/- mouse model of atherosclerosis using a clinical 3T MRI scanner to compare blood pool signal enhancement and specific accumulation in aortic plaques versus non-targeted Gd-liposomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for 'inverse targeting'—using vascular ligands to address blood cells rather than the vessel wall. It will contribute a new class of cell-hitchhiking contrast agents with potential for superior sensitivity in imaging inflammatory hotspots. The platform could later be adapted for targeted drug delivery to pathological leukocytes.
proposals.novelty: The inversion of the targeting paradigm from the static endothelium to mobile leukocytes is a non-obvious strategic shift. While ligand-targeted liposomes to endothelial cells exist, using those same ligands to create leukocyte-bound diagnostic agents represents a novel application of cell-mediated delivery within the vascular compartment.

proposals.title: Dual-Modality Iodine/Gadolinium-Loaded Liposomes with TAT-Penetration Peptides for MR/CT Imaging of Tumor Extravasation
proposals.motivation: Current long-circulating particulate contrast agents, such as gadolinium-loaded liposomes, are confined to the vascular lumen, limiting their utility to blood pool imaging. For diseases like cancer, where angiogenesis creates leaky vasculature, there is a critical unmet need for agents that can selectively extravasate and accumulate in diseased tissue, enabling precise morphological and functional imaging of the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that liposomes co-loaded with iodine (for CT) and gadolinium (for MRI), and surface-modified with cell-penetrating TAT peptides and long-circulating PEG, will selectively extravasate through the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in tumors, providing sustained, high-contrast imaging of extravascular tumor tissue.
proposals.method: We will engineer 100-120 nm liposomes using a lipid film hydration method, encapsulating iohexol (iodine-based) and Gd-DTPA-BMA within the aqueous core. Liposomes will be coated with DSPE-PEG(2000) for longevity and DSPE-PEG(2000)-TAT for tissue penetration. Physicochemical characterization (size, zeta potential, encapsulation efficiency) will be performed. In vitro extravasation will be tested using a endothelial cell monolayer model. In vivo efficacy will be evaluated in a murine xenograft tumor model using sequential MRI and CT imaging to quantify intratumoral accumulation versus blood pool signal over 24 hours.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, dual-modality nanoparticulate agent capable of moving beyond pure vascular imaging to delineate tumor margins and heterogeneity. It will establish a design principle for engineering 'leaky vasculature-seeking' particles, combining long circulation, controlled extravasation, and multi-modal imaging. This could significantly improve diagnostic accuracy and treatment monitoring in oncology.
proposals.novelty: While TAT-liposomes are known for intracellular delivery and gadolinium-liposomes for blood pool MRI, their combination for the explicit purpose of driving and imaging the extravasation of particulate agents into diseased tissue is novel. The dual iodine/gadolinium payload for correlated CT/MRI of the same extravascular target is also an innovative approach.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Extrusion and Post-Formulation Processing for Monodisperse, Long-Circulating Liposomes for Blood Pool Imaging
proposals.motivation: Uniform liposome size is critical for predictable pharmacokinetics, biodistribution, and efficacy in diagnostic and therapeutic applications, as demonstrated by Torchilin's work on long-circulating gadolinium-loaded liposomes for MRI. Pre-2026, achieving a narrow, reproducible size distribution remains a key challenge in liposome scale-up and clinical translation. Refining protocols to minimize polydispersity will directly enhance the performance and reliability of liposomal contrast agents and drug carriers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-parameter optimization of the post-hydration extrusion process, combined with a defined post-formation annealing step, will yield liposomes with a polydispersity index (PDI) consistently below 0.05, significantly improving upon current standard protocols.
proposals.method: We will employ a Design of Experiments (DoE) approach, systematically varying key extrusion parameters (temperature, pressure, number of passes through polycarbonate membranes of defined pore sizes from 50-200 nm) for DSPC/Cholesterol/PEG-DSPE liposomes. Post-extrusion, we will introduce and optimize a controlled annealing step at temperatures near the lipid phase transition. Size and PDI will be characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and corroborated by nanoparticle tracking analysis (NTA). The encapsulation efficiency of a model contrast agent (gadolinium-DTPA) will be monitored to ensure functionality is retained.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously optimized, step-by-step protocol for producing highly monodisperse, long-circulating liposomes, directly benefiting the development of next-generation blood pool MRI agents. It will establish clear process-property relationships for liposome size control, a valuable resource for translational nanomedicine. The resulting more uniform carriers are expected to exhibit more consistent in vivo behavior, improving diagnostic imaging reliability.
proposals.novelty: While extrusion is standard, the novelty lies in the systematic DoE optimization specifically targeting extreme monodispersity (PDI<0.05) for stealth liposomes, coupled with the investigation of a post-formation annealing step as a critical, yet often overlooked, protocol variable to achieve final size homogeneity.

proposals.title: Intracellular Fate and Metabolic Consequences of Disintegrated Long-Circulating Liposomal Components: A Study of Cellular Processing and Subacute Toxicity
proposals.motivation: Liposomal nanocarriers, including long-circulating PEGylated and cell-penetrating (e.g., TAT) variants, are widely used for drug and contrast agent delivery. While their pharmacokinetics and initial cellular uptake are well-studied, the long-term intracellular fate of their lipid and polymer components after carrier disintegration remains poorly understood. This gap is critical for assessing the safety of chronic or repeated therapeutic and diagnostic applications, particularly for blood-pool agents and intracellularly targeted systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intracellular disintegration of liposomes at approximately 9 hours post-uptake leads to the metabolic processing of their components (phospholipids, cholesterol, PEG-lipids) within lysosomal and cytoplasmic pathways, which may induce subacute cellular stress or adaptive responses without immediate cytotoxicity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize long-circulating, TAT-peptide-modified liposomes loaded with a dual-fluorescence reporter system: a pH-sensitive dye (e.g., CypHer5E) for tracking lysosomal escape/disintegration and a stable fluorophore (e.g., Alexa Fluor 647) in the bilayer. Endothelial cells and macrophages will be incubated with liposomes for 9 hours. Post-incubation, we will use confocal microscopy with organelle-specific stains and flow cytometry to track component redistribution. Cellular health will be assessed via assays for reactive oxygen species (ROS), mitochondrial membrane potential, and lysosomal membrane integrity at 12, 24, and 48 hours. Lipidomic analysis (LC-MS) will identify metabolic byproducts of the liposomal lipids.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed mechanistic map of the post-disintegration metabolic pathways for key liposomal excipients within cells. It will establish a foundational understanding of potential subacute biological effects, informing the design of safer, next-generation nanocarriers for repeated administration in therapy and diagnostic imaging.
proposals.novelty: While prior Torchilin-group work focused on liposome construction and initial targeting/imaging, this proposal shifts focus to the under-explored, later-stage intracellular metabolic sequelae. It uniquely links a specific disintegration timeline (9h) to downstream metabolic and stress responses, moving beyond standard acute cytotoxicity assays.

proposals.title: Development of a Long-Circulating Iodinated Liposomal Blood Pool Contrast Agent Targeted with Single-Domain Antibody Fragments
proposals.motivation: Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are widely used for targeting liposomal drug and contrast agents, but their large size (≈150 kDa) can limit tissue penetration and increase immunogenicity. This is particularly problematic for blood pool imaging agents, where rapid extravasation is undesirable but efficient endothelial targeting for molecular imaging is desired. Replacing full mAbs with smaller, high-affinity binding fragments could enhance the pharmacokinetic and safety profile of targeted liposomal carriers.
proposals.hypothesis: That liposomes decorated with single-domain antibody fragments (sdAbs, ≈15 kDa) against a vascular target (e.g., VCAM-1 or P-selectin) and loaded with an iodinated contrast agent will exhibit superior in vivo targeting efficiency and blood pool retention for computed tomography (CT) imaging compared to analogous full antibody-targeted liposomes, while reducing immunogenic potential.
proposals.method: We will engineer long-circulating PEGylated liposomes co-functionalized with anti-P-selectin sdAbs (via maleimide-thiol chemistry on terminal PEG) and loaded with a lipophilic iodine-based CT contrast agent (e.g., iohexol derivative). Liposome size, iodine payload, and sdAb density will be optimized. In vitro binding will be validated using activated endothelial cells. In vivo performance will be assessed in a murine inflammation model using micro-CT, comparing blood pool half-life and specific target accumulation against non-targeted and full mAb-targeted iodinated liposomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, head-to-head preclinical comparison of sdAb vs. full mAb targeting for liposomal blood pool contrast agents. It will establish a novel, potentially less immunogenic platform for molecular CT imaging of vascular biology and disease. The findings will inform the broader field of targeted nanomedicine on the trade-offs between ligand size and targeting efficacy.
proposals.novelty: While sdAbs have been explored in oncology, their application for targeting long-circulating *blood pool* liposomal contrast agents, specifically for CT imaging, is novel. The combination of Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating liposomes, iodine loading, and cell-penetrating peptides (like TAT) with the compact sdAb targeting modality represents a unique integration of pre-2026 technologies.

proposals.title: Closed-loop computational-experimental optimization of antibody-targeted long-circulating liposomes for solid tumor therapy
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research, the clinical translation of targeted liposomal drug carriers remains limited by suboptimal tumor delivery. This inefficiency stems from complex, competing in vivo processes—including the accelerated blood clearance (ABC) phenomenon, binding site barriers, and variable intracellular drug release—that are difficult to rationally balance using traditional trial-and-error formulation. A systematic, predictive framework is urgently needed to navigate this multi-parameter design space and achieve robust therapeutic efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mechanism-based pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model, iteratively calibrated with in vivo data, can accurately predict the tumor drug delivery efficiency of antibody-targeted liposomes and guide the selection of optimal formulation parameters (e.g., antibody density, PEG architecture).
proposals.method: We will develop a physiologically-based PK/PD model incorporating key processes: ABC phenomenon kinetics, target antigen binding, extravasation, and intracellular payload release. The model will be implemented in MATLAB/SimBiology and initially parameterized using in vitro binding data and in vivo biodistribution data from a small library of TAT- or antibody-conjugated liposomes loaded with a model drug. A global sensitivity analysis (Sobol method) will identify the most influential formulation parameters. An iterative, closed-loop cycle will then be established: the model will simulate tumor drug exposure for proposed designs, the top candidates will be synthesized and tested in a murine xenograft model, and the resulting data will be used to refine the model for the next design cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, predictive in silico platform for rational nanocarrier design, significantly accelerating the development of effective targeted therapies. It will also yield a novel, optimized antibody-targeted liposome formulation with demonstrated superior tumor delivery and a deeper mechanistic understanding of the interplay between stealth, targeting, and drug release in vivo.
proposals.novelty: While PK modeling of liposomes exists, the integration of a mechanism-based model capturing the ABC phenomenon and binding site barriers into a closed-loop, feedback-driven optimization platform for antibody-targeted liposome design is a novel and transformative approach. This moves beyond descriptive modeling to active, iterative design guidance.

proposals.title: In Vivo Biodistribution and Pharmacokinetics of Iodine-111-Labeled TAT-Modified Long-Circulating Liposomes: A Dual-Modality SPECT/CT and Blood Pool Contrast Study
proposals.motivation: Targeted liposomes, such as those modified with the TAT cell-penetrating peptide, hold immense promise for intracellular drug delivery. However, their in vivo fate—specifically, the balance between targeted tissue accumulation, non-specific biodistribution, and systemic clearance—remains inadequately characterized, limiting clinical translation. A comprehensive, quantitative assessment is needed to confirm targeting specificity and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TAT-modified long-circulating liposomes, radiolabeled with Iodine-111, will exhibit distinct biodistribution and pharmacokinetic profiles compared to non-targeted controls, showing enhanced accumulation in target tissues with high vascular permeability while maintaining favorable clearance kinetics.
proposals.method: We will prepare long-circulating PEGylated liposomes, with and without TAT-peptide conjugation, and label them with the gamma-emitting radionuclide Iodine-111 via a liposome-incorporated chelator for single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) imaging. A separate batch will be co-loaded with a gadolinium-based MR contrast agent. Biodistribution will be tracked longitudinally in tumor-bearing mouse models using SPECT/CT coregistration at 1, 4, 24, and 48 hours post-injection. Pharmacokinetic parameters (AUC, clearance, half-life) will be derived from serial blood sampling and gamma-counting, validated against concurrent non-invasive MR blood pool imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative dataset on the in vivo behavior of a clinically relevant targeted liposomal system. It will establish a robust dual-modality (nuclear/ MR) imaging protocol for correlating real-time whole-body distribution with blood pool kinetics, directly informing future liposome design for therapeutic applications.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's group has pioneered TAT-liposomes and gadolinium-loaded blood pool agents separately, this proposal uniquely integrates these concepts to perform simultaneous, multi-modal tracking of a single targeted carrier, enabling direct correlation of macro-distribution (SPECT) with vascular pharmacokinetics (MRI).

proposals.title: Site-Specific Conjugation of Antibodies to Long-Circulating Liposomes via Formylglycine-Mediated Oxime Ligation for Targeted Imaging and Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Immunoliposomes are powerful tools for targeted drug delivery and imaging, but conventional conjugation methods (e.g., via lysine or cysteine residues) yield heterogeneous populations with random antibody orientation. This significantly reduces the fraction of antibodies capable of binding target antigens, compromising targeting efficiency, batch reproducibility, and therapeutic efficacy. A method for generating homogeneous, uniformly oriented immunoliposomes is critically needed to realize their full clinical potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Genetically engineering an antibody to incorporate a formylglycine-generating peptide (FGE-recognition sequence) in its Fc region will enable site-specific installation of a bioorthogonal aldehyde handle. This will allow for the efficient, oriented conjugation of the antibody to hydrazine-functionalized liposomes via oxime ligation, producing immunoliposomes with superior and more consistent target-binding capacity compared to those made by stochastic methods.
proposals.method: We will engineer a human IgG1 antibody with an FGE-recognition sequence (LCTPSR) inserted into a permissive loop of the Fc region. The purified antibody will be treated with recombinant formylglycine-generating enzyme (FGE) to convert the cysteine in the tag to formylglycine (fGly), creating a unique aldehyde handle. In parallel, we will prepare long-circulating PEGylated liposomes (similar to our gadolinium-loaded blood-pool agents) functionalized with hydrazine-PEG-DSPE. The fGly-modified antibody will be conjugated to the liposomes via catalyst-free oxime ligation. Conjugation efficiency, antibody orientation, and liposome stability will be characterized using SDS-PAGE, ELISA, and dynamic light scattering.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, genetically encoded platform for creating uniformly oriented immunoliposomes, directly addressing a major translational bottleneck. It will provide a generalizable conjugation strategy applicable to any antibody, enhancing the targeting precision of liposomal carriers for both therapeutic (e.g., TAT-liposome derivatives) and diagnostic (e.g., blood-pool imaging) applications pioneered in our lab.
proposals.novelty: While bioorthogonal chemistry for antibody conjugation is known, integrating the genetically encoded FGE/fGly system specifically for the oriented conjugation of full-length antibodies to pre-formed, clinically relevant long-circulating liposomes represents a novel and rational design not yet demonstrated in the immunoliposome field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Site-Specific Antibody Conjugation to Long-Circulating Liposomes via Strain-Promoted Azide-Alkyne Cycloaddition (SPAAC) for Enhanced Blood-Pool Targeting
proposals.motivation: Antibody-conjugated liposomes are promising for targeted drug delivery and imaging, but conventional conjugation methods (e.g., carbodiimide chemistry) are often inefficient, non-specific, and can compromise antibody or liposome integrity. This leads to heterogeneous products with suboptimal targeting and pharmacokinetics. A robust, bioorthogonal method is needed to create well-defined immunoliposomes with preserved function, particularly for applications like blood-pool imaging where consistent surface properties dictate circulation time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition (SPAAC), a catalyst-free click chemistry, for site-specific conjugation of antibodies to PEGylated liposomes will yield immunoliposomes with superior conjugation efficiency, batch-to-batch consistency, and in vivo targeting performance compared to those prepared via traditional amine-coupling methods.
proposals.method: DSPE-PEG(2000)-cyclooctyne (DBCO) will be incorporated into the bilayer of long-circulating, gadolinium-loaded liposomes (based on Torchilin's prior work). Model antibodies (e.g., anti-ICAM-1) will be site-specifically modified with an azide linker using enzymatic glycan remodeling (via Fc-specific glycosyltransferases) or engineered cysteine residues. Conjugation will be performed by incubating azide-modified antibodies with DBCO-functionalized liposomes. Conjugation efficiency and antibody orientation will be quantified via gel electrophoresis and ELISA. The targeting efficacy and blood-pool retention of the resulting immunoliposomes will be evaluated in vitro using activated endothelial cells and in vivo in a murine model using magnetic resonance imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a standardized, high-efficiency platform for generating homogeneous antibody-liposome conjugates. It will directly advance the field of targeted nanomedicine by providing a method to improve the reproducibility and functional performance of immunoliposomes. The findings will be particularly impactful for developing next-generation targeted contrast agents and therapeutics with predictable pharmacokinetics.
proposals.novelty: While click chemistry has been explored in nanomaterials, its application for the site-specific, Fc-region directed conjugation of full antibodies to clinically relevant long-circulating (PEGylated) liposomes represents a significant methodological advance over random lysine coupling, offering precise control over antibody orientation and density that is not achieved with pre-2026 standard techniques.

proposals.title: pH- and Cathepsin-B-Activated AND-Gate Liposomes for Intracellularly Targeted Drug Delivery
proposals.motivation: Liposomal drug delivery systems, including TAT-modified carriers, often suffer from premature drug release in the bloodstream or non-specific endosomal escape, leading to off-target effects and reduced therapeutic efficacy. While single-stimulus responsive liposomes (e.g., pH-sensitive) have been developed, they lack the specificity to differentiate between various mildly acidic compartments, limiting their reliability for pathway-specific activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering liposomes with a dual-stimuli AND-gate release mechanism, requiring the sequential action of endosomal acidification and a specific endo-lysosomal protease, will provide a more precise and reliable trigger for intracellular drug release, minimizing off-target effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a stabilizing peptide-lipid conjugate featuring a pH-sensitive histidine-glutamate (HE) sequence linked to a cathepsin-B cleavable peptide (e.g., Gly-Phe-Leu-Gly). This conjugate will be incorporated into long-circulating PEGylated liposomes. At neutral pH, the conjugate stabilizes the bilayer. In acidic endosomes, the HE sequence protonates, inducing a conformational change that exposes the cleavage site. Only subsequent cleavage by cathepsin-B will disrupt the conjugate, destabilizing the liposome and releasing the encapsulated cargo (e.g., a fluorescent dye or model drug). Release kinetics and specificity will be validated using fluorescence dequenching assays in vitro under conditions mimicking extracellular, early endosomal, and late endosomal/lysosomal environments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel class of logic-gated nanocarriers that significantly enhance the specificity of intracellular drug delivery. It will provide a robust platform for delivering sensitive therapeutics (e.g., proteins, nucleic acids) with high spatial and pathway fidelity, directly building upon Torchilin's prior work on long-circulating and intracellularly targeted liposomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the sequential, AND-logic biological trigger (pH *then* enzyme) implemented via a single molecular construct, moving beyond single-stimulus or additive dual-stimulus systems to achieve superior compartmental specificity for endosomal release.

proposals.title: Endothelium-targeted multi-compartment liposomes co-delivering doxorubicin and anti-IL-6 siRNA for dual therapy in inflammatory breast cancer
proposals.motivation: Inflammatory cancers, such as inflammatory breast cancer (IBC), are driven by a pathological tumor microenvironment (TME) featuring aberrant cytokine signaling and dysfunctional vasculature, which promotes metastasis and therapy resistance. Pre-2026, liposomal doxorubicin (e.g., Doxil®) improves pharmacokinetics but lacks specific TME targeting and does not address the pro-tumorigenic inflammatory cascade. A delivery system that simultaneously targets tumor cells and the inflammatory vasculature is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that endothelium-targeted, multi-compartment liposomes co-encapsulating a cytotoxic drug (doxorubicin) and an anti-inflammatory biotherapeutic (siRNA against IL-6) will synergistically enhance anti-tumor efficacy in inflammatory cancer models by concurrently inducing cancer cell death and normalizing the pathological tumor vasculature and microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will construct PEGylated liposomes with an RGD-peptide ligand for targeting αvβ3 integrin on tumor endothelium. Using a double-emulsion or sequential loading technique, we will create a multi-compartment system: the lipid bilayer will encapsulate doxorubicin in the aqueous core, while siRNA against interleukin-6 (IL-6) will be complexed with cationic lipids or polymers within a distinct, separate compartment of the same vesicle. The platform will be characterized for size, encapsulation efficiency, and ligand density. Efficacy and mechanism will be evaluated in vitro using activated endothelial and IBC cell co-cultures, and in vivo using a relevant orthotopic IBC mouse model, assessing tumor growth, metastasis, and changes in vascular permeability and inflammatory markers via histology and molecular assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, targeted combinatorial nanomedicine platform designed to treat the dual components of inflammatory cancer. It will provide proof-of-concept that coordinated vascular targeting and modulation of the TME can overcome delivery barriers and resistance. The findings will advance the field of liposomal drug delivery from single-agent carriers to sophisticated, multi-functional systems for complex diseases.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's lab has pioneered long-circulating and TAT-modified liposomes, this proposal uniquely integrates a specific vascular targeting ligand with a physically partitioned co-delivery system for a cytotoxic drug and an anti-inflammatory nucleic acid, enabling a precise, dual-pathway intervention not achieved by simple mixture or single-compartment carriers.

proposals.title: Targeted Polymeric Micelles for Enhanced Myocardial Delivery: Balancing Long Circulation with Active αvβ3-Integrin Targeting
proposals.motivation: Long-circulating 'stealth' nanocarriers, such as PEGylated liposomes and micelles, achieve passive tumor accumulation via the EPR effect but show limited efficacy for many non-cancer targets, including ischemic myocardium. Active targeting ligands can enhance specific tissue uptake, but their addition often compromises the 'stealth' properties essential for prolonged blood pool residence, a trade-off not systematically quantified for cardiac applications. This project addresses the critical need to optimize this balance for therapeutic delivery to the heart.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by conjugating a cyclic RGD peptide (cRGDfK) targeting αvβ3-integrin, which is upregulated on angiogenic endothelial cells in ischemic myocardium, to the surface of long-circulating PEG-PE micelles, we can achieve superior myocardial accumulation compared to passive micelles, despite a potential reduction in circulation half-life, resulting in enhanced delivery of a model therapeutic or diagnostic payload.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PEG(2000)-PE micelles loaded with a model agent (e.g., a near-infrared dye or a cardioprotective drug like adenosine). A portion will be functionalized with cRGDfK via a terminal maleimide group on a fraction of the PEG chains. Using pre-2026 established methods, we will characterize size, loading, and ligand density. Pharmacokinetics and biodistribution will be quantitatively compared in a rat model of myocardial ischemia-reperfusion using optical imaging or radiolabeling. Specific accumulation in the infarct border zone will be validated ex vivo via fluorescence microscopy and correlated with hemodynamic measurements of cardiac function.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for the ligand-density-dependent trade-off between circulation time and active targeting efficiency for a clinically relevant cardiac target. It will advance the field of targeted nanomedicine beyond oncology by demonstrating a rationally designed micelle for myocardial delivery. The findings will guide the development of next-generation theranostic agents for cardiovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Torchilin's lab has pioneered long-circulating blood-pool agents (e.g., gadolinium liposomes) and intracellular delivery (TAT-liposomes), applying active targeting principles to polymeric micelles for myocardial ischemia is a distinct and underexplored application. The systematic study of how ligand grafting density on a micelle platform affects the critical balance between stealth and targeting in this context is novel.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetics and Target Site Accumulation of Radiolabeled PEG-immunoliposomes in a Porcine Model: A Preclinical Bridge to Human Applications
proposals.motivation: Preclinical evaluation of nanocarriers like PEG-immunoliposomes has predominantly relied on rodent models, which often fail to accurately predict human pharmacokinetics due to significant physiological and anatomical differences. This translational gap is a major bottleneck in the clinical development of targeted liposomal therapeutics and diagnostics. Assessing these advanced carriers in a large animal model with circulatory and metabolic parameters closer to humans is a critical, unmet step prior to first-in-human trials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a large animal model (swine), PEG-immunoliposomes will demonstrate prolonged circulation times and superior, antigen-specific targeting to endothelial markers compared to non-targeted PEG-liposomes, with pharmacokinetic parameters that more closely approximate projected human profiles than rodent data.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes co-loaded with a gamma-emitting radioisotope (e.g., I-125 for SPECT imaging or In-111 for SPECT/CT) and a fluorescent lipid marker. Immunoliposomes will be functionalized with monoclonal antibodies against a porcine-specific endothelial antigen (e.g., ICAM-1). Pharmacokinetics (blood clearance, AUC, Vd) will be rigorously quantified via serial blood sampling and gamma counting in healthy swine. Concurrent non-invasive SPECT/CT imaging will visualize real-time biodistribution. Ex vivo gamma counting of harvested organs and fluorescence microscopy of tissue sections will quantify target site accumulation and cellular localization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the in vivo behavior of actively targeted immunoliposomes in a physiologically relevant large animal model. It will establish critical scaling parameters for liposomal circulation half-life and biodistribution, directly informing the design of future clinical trials. The study will validate the feasibility of using immunoliposomes for vascular targeting in a human-scale circulatory system.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Torchilin's group has pioneered long-circulating and TAT-modified liposomes, their systematic evaluation in a large animal model for quantitative pharmacokinetics and simultaneous imaging-based targeting assessment is novel. This proposal integrates his expertise in liposome engineering, blood-pool agents, and imaging to address a key translational challenge not yet resolved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Molecular Dynamics-Guided Optimization of Antibody Conformation on PEGylated Liposomes for Enhanced Target Binding
proposals.motivation: Liposomes functionalized with antibodies (immunoliposomes) are a powerful platform for targeted drug delivery and imaging, as evidenced by Torchilin's prior work on TAT-liposomes and long-circulating contrast agents. However, the presentation of antibodies on the sterically protective PEG corona is suboptimal; PEG chains can sterically hinder antibody-antigen binding, reducing targeting efficacy. Pre-2026, empirical trial-and-error dominates surface optimization, lacking a molecular-level understanding of PEG-antibody interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic molecular dynamics (MD) simulations can identify optimal PEG chain length, density, and antibody conjugation chemistry that minimizes steric interference, thereby maximizing the antigen-binding fragment (Fab) accessibility and orientation for efficient target recognition.
proposals.method: We will employ all-atom and coarse-grained MD simulations using GROMACS and the Martini force field. Models will include a DPPC/cholesterol liposome bilayer grafted with PEG (MW 1000-5000 Da) at varying densities, conjugated to a model antibody (e.g., trastuzumab Fab fragment) via maleimide-thiol or click chemistry linkers. Simulations will analyze Fab orientation, solvent-accessible surface area, and interaction energies with a modeled target antigen extracellular domain. Key metrics include the Fab's radial distribution and angular freedom relative to the liposome surface.
