proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computational framework for rationally designing immunoliposome surfaces, moving beyond empirical formulations. It will yield specific design rules for PEG-antibody architectures that balance longevity (from PEG) and targeting efficiency. The validated models will directly inform the synthesis of next-generation targeted liposomes for drug delivery and molecular imaging, such as antibody-directed blood-pool contrast agents.
proposals.novelty: While MD has been used to study plain or PEGylated liposomes, its application to explicitly model and optimize the conformational dynamics of a full antibody fragment within a dense PEG brush for targeted delivery is novel. This integrates Torchilin's expertise in liposome engineering with computational biophysics to solve a critical bottleneck in immunoliposome design.

proposals.title: Targeted Sub-50 nm PEGylated Liposomes for Enhanced Penetration and Imaging of the Myocardial Infarct Border Zone
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, myocardial infarction (MI) diagnosis and treatment monitoring rely on imaging agents with limited ability to penetrate the dense, ischemic tissue of the infarct border zone. While long-circulating, PEGylated liposomes (e.g., Torchilin's gadolinium-loaded blood pool agents) provide excellent vascular contrast, their large size (>100 nm) hinders extravasation and diffusion into this critical region, limiting diagnostic sensitivity and targeted therapeutic delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering antibody-targeted, PEG-coated liposomes with a diameter below 50 nm will significantly improve their diffusion and accumulation in the infarct border zone compared to standard >100 nm agents, due to enhanced extravasation and interstitial penetration.
proposals.method: We will fabricate monoclonal anti-myosin antibody-conjugated PEG-liposomes via post-insertion of PEG-lipid-antibody conjugates into pre-formed liposomes. Liposome size will be rigorously controlled to sub-50 nm using extrusion through polycarbonate membranes (e.g., 30 nm pores) and characterized by dynamic light scattering. These nanocarriers will be loaded with a pre-2026 clinically relevant contrast agent (iodine for CT or gadolinium for MRI). Their targeting efficacy and penetration depth will be evaluated in an isolated rat heart model of global ischemia, comparing them to non-targeted and larger (>100 nm) targeted counterparts using imaging and biodistribution analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform of ultra-small, targeted liposomal contrast agents designed specifically for penetrating fibrotic cardiac tissue. It will provide critical proof-of-concept data on the relationship between nanoparticle size, targeting, and penetration in ischemic heart disease, informing future theranostic strategies for MI.
proposals.novelty: While Torchilin's prior work established long-circulating blood pool liposomes and intracellular delivery (TAT-liposomes), this proposal uniquely combines extreme size reduction (<50 nm) with antibody targeting specifically to overcome diffusion barriers in the infarct microenvironment, a challenge not addressed by previous blood-pool agents.

proposals.title: Quantifying Active vs. Passive Tumor Accumulation: A Systematic Comparison of Ligand-Targeted and Non-Targeted PEGylated Liposomal Contrast Agents
proposals.motivation: Targeted liposomal drug carriers and imaging agents are widely studied for cancer therapy and diagnosis. However, the precise contribution of active, receptor-mediated uptake versus passive accumulation via the Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR) effect remains poorly quantified. This ambiguity hinders the rational design and optimization of targeted nanocarriers, as the true benefit of the targeting ligand is often unclear.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a direct, systematic comparison of targeted liposomes to their non-targeted, PEGylated counterparts, using identical physicochemical properties and a blood-pool imaging readout, will allow for the precise quantification of ligand-specific, active cellular uptake versus passive, EPR-mediated accumulation in tumors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize two liposomal formulations with identical lipid composition, size (~100 nm), PEG coating, and iodine-based blood-pool contrast agent (as used in Torchilin's prior work). The 'targeted' liposome will be functionalized with the TAT cell-penetrating peptide (a well-established model from the PI's work). The 'non-targeted control' will be an identical PEGylated liposome without TAT. Using a pre-clinical tumor model, we will perform longitudinal computed tomography (CT) imaging to quantify blood-pool and tumor pharmacokinetics. Ex vivo analysis will measure total tumor accumulation and, via flow cytometry and confocal microscopy of dissociated tumors, quantify the fraction of liposomes internalized by different cell populations (tumor cells, macrophages).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust methodological framework for deconvoluting the mechanisms of nanocarrier tumor delivery. It will yield quantitative benchmarks for the added value of a specific targeting ligand (TAT) in a relevant model. The findings will inform future design rules for targeted liposomes, distinguishing between formulations that benefit from active targeting versus those where passive accumulation suffices.
proposals.novelty: While many studies include a 'non-targeted' control, a systematic, isochemical comparison focused on quantifying cellular internalization mechanisms using a blood-pool imaging agent for precise pharmacokinetic analysis is not standard. This approach leverages the PI's expertise in long-circulating and intracellular carriers to create a definitive control for active targeting studies.

proposals.title: Stimuli-Responsive Mobilization of Sub-200 nm and Supra-200 nm Liposomes from the Blood Pool: Redefining Optimal Size for Targeted Delivery
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including Torchilin's own on long-circulating blood-pool agents, established ~200 nm as an optimal liposome size for passive stability and biodistribution. However, this paradigm may limit active, on-demand delivery strategies. If different stimuli (e.g., enzymatic, thermal, pH) can selectively mobilize liposomes of specific sizes from circulation, the 'optimal' size becomes context-dependent, opening new avenues for spatiotemporal control in drug delivery and imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that liposomes smaller (<120 nm) and larger (>300 nm) than the conventional 200 nm benchmark can be designed to remain passively stable in circulation but be actively and more effectively mobilized into target tissues by distinct external or pathophysiological stimuli compared to 200 nm counterparts.
proposals.method: We will fabricate PEGylated liposomes of three distinct sizes (80 nm, 200 nm, 350 nm) loaded with a gadolinium-based MRI contrast agent for real-time blood-pool tracking. These liposomes will be engineered with stimuli-sensitive linkers (e.g., matrix metalloproteinase-2 (MMP-2) cleavable PEG or pH-sensitive phosphatidylethanolamine) on their surface. Using a pre-2026 isolated rat heart model of regional ischemia/reperfusion, we will quantify the mobilization efficiency of each size cohort from the perfusate (blood pool analog) into the ischemic myocardium in response to the specific stimulus (elevated MMP-2 or acidic pH), measured via T1-weighted MRI signal change and gamma-counting of a co-encapsulated radiotracer.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond a single 'optimal' size by establishing a stimulus-size relationship, providing a rational design framework for next-generation responsive nanocarriers. It will directly contribute to the development of more precise therapeutic and diagnostic strategies where delivery is triggered by disease-specific signals rather than passive accumulation.
proposals.novelty: While stimuli-sensitive liposomes exist, systematically testing the hypothesis that sub- and supra-optimal passive sizes offer superior active mobilization potential is novel. It challenges the static 200 nm dogma by introducing a dynamic, stimulus-dependent definition of optimality.

proposals.title: Impact of Lipopolysaccharide Presentation on Liposomal Adjuvants: Evaluating the Immunogenicity and Systemic Toxicity of Surface-Adsorbed versus Fully-Incorporated Antigens
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a potent immunostimulant but induces severe systemic toxicity (e.g., septic shock). Pre-2026 work by Torchilin's group and others has shown that encapsulating LPS within long-circulating liposomes can significantly reduce its toxicity while preserving adjuvant activity for vaccine development. However, the necessity of full encapsulation, a complex and potentially inefficient process, has not been rigorously challenged. Determining if simpler preparation methods (e.g., surface adsorption) yield a similar therapeutic index would streamline adjuvant liposome production.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the physical state of LPS association with liposomes (surface-adsorbed vs. fully incorporated into the bilayer) critically determines the balance between systemic toxicity and local immunogenicity, with surface-adsorbed forms potentially offering a comparable safety profile to encapsulated forms while maintaining adjuvant potency.
proposals.method: We will prepare three distinct formulations of LPS-associated PEGylated liposomes: 1) LPS fully incorporated into the lipid bilayer during film hydration, 2) LPS adsorbed onto pre-formed liposome surfaces via charge or hydrophobic interaction, and 3) a control of free LPS. Using an established murine model, we will quantify systemic toxicity via serum cytokine (TNF-α, IL-6) levels and clinical signs. Immunogenicity will be assessed by co-administering formulations with a model antigen (ovalbumin) and measuring antigen-specific IgG titers and T-cell responses. In vitro, we will use RAW 264.7 macrophage cells to compare TLR4 activation and cytokine secretion profiles between formulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-activity relationship for LPS-based liposomal adjuvants, directly linking preparation method to biological outcome. It will provide concrete formulation guidelines for vaccine adjuvant development, potentially identifying a simpler, more scalable production method that retains efficacy with low toxicity. The findings will advance the rational design of safer immunomodulatory nanocarriers.
proposals.novelty: While the detoxification effect of encapsulated LPS is known, systematically comparing it to surface-adsorbed states is novel. This challenges the prevailing assumption that complete sequestration is mandatory for safety, exploring a potentially more practical adjuvant platform.

proposals.title: Engineering Hydrophilic Lipopolysaccharide Salts for Stable and Nontoxic Liposomal Formulations as Immunomodulatory Agents
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS), a potent immunostimulant, is inherently hydrophobic, complicating its stable incorporation into liposomal carriers for controlled immunotherapy. Pre-2026, Torchilin's lab has pioneered long-circulating and intracellularly targeted liposomes for drug and contrast agent delivery. Developing a method to formulate hydrophilic LPS salts would enable the creation of novel, safer liposomal adjuvants or immunotherapies with reduced toxicity and improved pharmacokinetics, leveraging the lab's expertise in blood-pool imaging and targeted delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: That specific hydrophilic salt forms of LPS (e.g., with divalent cations or complexed with hydrophilic polymers) can be engineered to allow stable and efficient encapsulation within liposomal bilayers while significantly reducing the inherent toxicity associated with free LPS aggregates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize hydrophilic LPS salts by complexing LPS with cations (e.g., Ca2+, Mg2+) or polyethylene glycol (PEG)-based polymers. These salts will be incorporated into liposomes using modified lipid film hydration or remote loading techniques, building on the lab's experience with TAT- and gadolinium-loaded liposomes. Formulations will be characterized for size, zeta potential, encapsulation efficiency, and in vitro stability. Toxicity will be assessed in macrophage cell lines (e.g., RAW 264.7) via cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6) and cell viability assays, compared to free LPS. Immunostimulatory potency will be evaluated using NF-κB reporter assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel formulation paradigm for incorporating a key but challenging immunomodulator into liposomal nanocarriers. It will contribute a new class of engineered LPS-liposome complexes with a potentially improved safety profile for research in vaccine adjuvants or cancer immunotherapy. The findings will expand the toolkit for liposomal delivery of amphiphilic biological response modifiers.
proposals.novelty: While hydrophobic LPS incorporation is known, the deliberate engineering of hydrophilic LPS salts specifically for stable liposomal formulation is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines LPS chemistry modification with advanced liposome technology to directly address the toxicity and formulation instability bottlenecks.

proposals.title: Characterization of the Humoral Immune Response: Antibody Isotype Profiles Induced by Liposomal Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) Vaccine Formulations
proposals.motivation: Liposomal encapsulation of bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a promising strategy for developing vaccines against Gram-negative pathogens, as it can detoxify LPS while preserving immunogenicity. However, the quality of the humoral response, defined by the induced antibody isotype and subclass profile, remains poorly characterized. A detailed isotype analysis is critical because it determines effector functions such as complement activation, opsonophagocytosis, and mucosal immunity, which are essential for vaccine efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: Immunization with liposomal LPS formulations will induce a distinct, Th1-biased antibody isotype profile (e.g., elevated IgG2a in mice, correlating to IgG1 in humans) compared to free or standard adjuvant-formulated LPS, due to altered biodistribution and enhanced delivery to antigen-presenting cells.
proposals.method: We will prepare liposomes using Dr. Torchilin's established long-circulating PEGylated lipid compositions and encapsulate purified E. coli LPS. Mice will be immunized subcutaneously with liposomal LPS, free LPS, or LPS with alum. Serum will be collected at intervals. Antigen-specific antibody titers and isotypes (IgM, IgG1, IgG2a, IgG2b, IgG3, IgA) will be quantified using ELISA with LPS-coated plates and isotype-specific secondary antibodies. The data will be analyzed to compare the magnitude and isotype skew of the responses between formulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational immunochemical profile of the response to a key liposomal vaccine candidate, linking formulation to immune outcome. It will establish whether liposomal delivery can steer the humoral response towards a more protective isotype profile, informing the rational design of next-generation antibacterial vaccines. The findings will directly complement Dr. Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating liposomes for imaging and drug delivery by applying it to vaccinology.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Torchilin's group has extensively studied liposomes for drug delivery (e.g., TAT-liposomes) and imaging (e.g., gadolinium-loaded blood pool agents), applying this platform to systematically deconstruct the humoral response to an LPS vaccine is a novel intersection of his delivery expertise and immunology. Prior work focused on liposomal LPS often assessed only total IgG or protection, not a comprehensive isotype analysis.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Long-Circulating Liposome Lipid Composition for Enhanced Adjuvant Activity Against Lipopolysaccharide
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a potent bacterial endotoxin responsible for severe inflammatory responses and sepsis. While liposomes are established as vaccine adjuvants and drug carriers, their immunostimulatory properties are highly dependent on physicochemical composition. Pre-2026, Torchilin's lab has pioneered long-circulating liposomes (e.g., PEGylated) for imaging and delivery, but a systematic study to engineer their lipid makeup specifically to amplify the anti-LPS immune response is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically varying the molar ratios of key lipid components (cholesterol, phospholipid saturation, and PEG-lipid content) in long-circulating liposomes will identify an optimal composition that maximizes adjuvant activity, leading to a stronger and more specific humoral immune response against LPS.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a library of liposomes using a design-of-experiments (DoE) approach, varying cholesterol (20-45 mol%), phospholipid chain saturation (DSPC, DOPC, DPPC), and PEG2000-DSPE (0-10 mol%). Liposomes will be characterized for size, zeta potential, and stability. The adjuvant effect will be tested in a murine model by co-administering each liposome formulation with an LPS antigen. The primary readout will be serum anti-LPS IgG titers measured by ELISA, with secondary analyses of IgG subtypes and cytokine profiles to characterize the T-helper bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-activity relationship between liposome lipid composition and adjuvant potency against a critical bacterial target. It will yield a rationally optimized, long-circulating liposome adjuvant platform that could be applied to sepsis vaccine development or immunotherapies. The findings will extend the utility of blood-pool liposomes from passive carriers to active immunomodulators.
proposals.novelty: While liposome adjuvants exist, this proposal uniquely applies Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating, PEGylated liposome engineering—traditionally for imaging and drug delivery—to a systematic, multi-parameter optimization for anti-LPS immunogenicity, a targeted application not previously explored in his pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Comparative Analysis of Dehydration-Rehydration and Cosonication for Liposomal Incorporation of Diverse Lipopolysaccharide Chemotypes
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) are potent immunostimulants and key targets for vaccine adjuvants and immunotherapy. However, their integration into stable, biocompatible carriers like liposomes is complicated by their structural diversity, particularly in the hydrophobic lipid A and core oligosaccharide regions. Pre-2026, methods for LPS encapsulation have been largely optimized for single chemotypes, creating a gap in understanding how universal these techniques are across different bacterial species, which limits the development of broad-spectrum or species-specific liposomal immunotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the efficiency and stability of LPS incorporation into liposomes are critically dependent on the LPS chemotype (specifically lipid A acyl chain number and core polysaccharide length) and that the dehydration-rehydration method will be superior for LPS with longer core oligosaccharides, while cosonication will be more effective for rough-type LPS with minimal core structures.
proposals.method: We will isolate or source purified LPS from E. coli (smooth, hexa-acylated), Salmonella minnesota Re595 (rough, tetra-acylated), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (smooth, hexa-acylated with distinct core). For each LPS, we will prepare liposomes using two established pre-2026 methods: (1) Dehydration-rehydration vesicles (DRV), involving co-dissolution of LPS with phospholipids (e.g., DPPC/cholesterol) in organic solvent, followed by hydration, freeze-drying, and rehydration. (2) Cosonication, where a mixed LPS/phospholipid dispersion is probe-sonicated. Incorporation efficiency will be quantified via a modified Limulus Amebocyte Lysate (LAL) assay on separated liposomes, and vesicle stability/size will be characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and negative stain electron microscopy pre- and post-serum incubation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a systematic, comparative dataset linking LPS chemical structure to optimal liposomal formulation parameters. It will provide a practical methodological framework for researchers aiming to encapsulate novel LPS variants for immunological studies or adjuvant development. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective liposome-based vaccine carriers and immunomodulatory agents targeting specific pathogen-associated molecular patterns.
proposals.novelty: While liposomal encapsulation of drugs and antigens is well-established in Torchilin's and others' work, a direct, systematic comparison of foundational physical incorporation methods across structurally defined LPS chemotypes from diverse pathogens has not been conducted pre-2026. This moves beyond single-model studies to establish structure-encapsulation relationships.

proposals.title: Long-Term Humoral and Cellular Memory Induced by Liposomal Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) Vaccines: A 90-Day Immunogenicity Study
proposals.motivation: Liposomal carriers, pioneered by researchers like Torchilin, enhance drug and antigen delivery but their long-term immunogenic profile for potent antigens like LPS is poorly characterized. Understanding the durability of the immune response is critical for vaccine development against Gram-negative pathogens, yet most studies focus on early-phase immunity (<21 days). This gap limits the translational potential of liposome-based adjuvants and vaccines.
proposals.hypothesis: Liposomal encapsulation of LPS will induce a sustained, high-titer antibody response and generate a robust pool of antigen-specific memory B cells that persists for at least 90 days post-immunization, superior to free LPS or standard alum-adjuvanted formulations.
proposals.method: We will formulate LPS within long-circulating PEGylated liposomes, using techniques from Torchilin's work on blood-pool agents. Mice will be immunized subcutaneously, and serum collected at days 0, 21, 60, and 90 for ELISA-based anti-LPS IgG/IgM titer analysis. At day 90, splenocytes will be isolated; memory B cells will be quantified via flow cytometry using markers (CD19+, CD38+, IgG+) and a fluorescent LPS probe. An ELISpot assay for LPS-specific antibody-secreting cells will assess the functional memory pool.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a comprehensive, longitudinal dataset on the immunogenicity of liposomal LPS, defining its kinetic profile. It will establish whether liposomal delivery converts a typically transient or toxic LPS response into a durable, safe immunological memory, informing the design of next-generation antibacterial and antitoxin vaccines.
proposals.novelty: While liposomes are known as intracellular carriers and contrast agents, their systematic evaluation for inducing long-term B-cell memory to a model pathogen-associated molecular pattern (PAMP) like LPS is novel. The proposal uniquely combines Torchilin's liposome engineering with advanced immunophenotyping to bridge nanomedicine and vaccinology.

proposals.title: Controlled Reconstitution of Lipopolysaccharide into Long-Circulating Liposomes via Detergent Dialysis for Advanced Blood Pool Imaging and Sepsis Modeling
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a potent immune stimulant, and its controlled incorporation into liposomes is crucial for creating advanced models of sepsis and for developing targeted imaging agents for inflammatory sites. Pre-2026 methods for LPS-liposome preparation, such as simple film hydration or extrusion, often yield heterogeneous and unstable formulations with poor control over LPS orientation and density. This limits their utility in precise biomedical applications where consistent immune activation or targeting is required.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the detergent dialysis method, commonly used for the gentle reconstitution of membrane proteins, will enable the controlled, reproducible, and scalable incorporation of LPS into long-circulating liposomes, resulting in stable, homogeneous vesicles with defined immunostimulatory and targeting properties.
proposals.method: We will employ a detergent removal technique using octyl glucoside or sodium cholate. LPS will be mixed with phospholipids (e.g., HSPC, cholesterol, and PEG-DSPE for longevity) in a detergent solution to form mixed micelles. Controlled removal of the detergent via dialysis against a large buffer volume will drive the spontaneous formation of unilamellar liposomes with LPS quantitatively and asymmetrically incorporated into the bilayer. The process will be optimized by varying the LPS-to-lipid ratio, detergent type, and dialysis conditions. Resulting liposomes will be characterized for size (DLS), lamellarity (cryo-EM), LPS incorporation efficiency (Limulus amebocyte lysate assay, fluorescent LPS analogs), and in vitro stability in serum.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, novel platform for creating well-defined LPS-liposome complexes, advancing the field of immunoliposomes. It will contribute a critical tool for creating more accurate in vitro and in vivo models of septic shock. Furthermore, it will provide a foundation for developing next-generation liposomal contrast agents that target the vascular endothelium in inflammatory diseases, building upon Torchilin's prior work with blood pool imaging agents.
proposals.novelty: While detergent dialysis is a standard in membrane protein biochemistry, its application for the controlled reconstitution of complex glycolipids like LPS into sterically stabilized, long-circulating liposomes is novel. This approach moves beyond simple physical adsorption or encapsulation, allowing precise engineering of the LPS-lipid bilayer interface for the first time.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Profiling of Liposomal Lipopolysaccharide Immunomodulation Using Toll-like Receptor Reporter Cell Assays
proposals.motivation: Liposomal encapsulation of potent immunostimulants like lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a promising strategy to modulate inflammatory responses for therapeutic applications, such as in vaccine adjuvants or cancer immunotherapy. However, the precise molecular mechanisms by which liposome formulation attenuates LPS-induced signaling through Toll-like Receptor 4 (TLR4) pathways remain poorly characterized. A systematic, mechanistic understanding is crucial for the rational design of safer and more effective liposomal immunomodulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that liposomal encapsulation of LPS significantly dampens pro-inflammatory signaling by altering the kinetics and magnitude of TLR4/MyD88 and TLR4/TRIF pathway activation, which can be quantitatively dissected using isogenic TLR reporter cell lines.
proposals.method: We will employ commercially available HEK293-derived reporter cell lines stably transfected with human TLR4, CD14, and MD-2, along with NF-κB or IRF-responsive luciferase constructs to separately monitor the MyD88-dependent and TRIF-dependent signaling branches. Liposomal LPS will be prepared using established lipid compositions (e.g., DSPC/Cholesterol/PEG-lipid) from Torchilin's prior work on long-circulating carriers. Dose-response and time-course experiments will quantify luciferase activity, comparing free LPS to liposomal LPS. Parallel experiments with inhibitors of endocytosis (e.g., chlorpromazine) will determine the role of cellular uptake pathways in the observed signaling profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, branch-specific analysis of how liposomal formulation modulates TLR4 signaling, moving beyond simple cytokine measurements. It will establish a robust in vitro screening platform for predicting the immunotoxicity of novel liposomal agents. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation liposomal carriers for immunomodulatory drugs, balancing efficacy with reduced systemic inflammatory risk.
proposals.novelty: While liposomal LPS is known to be less toxic, the application of isogenic, pathway-specific TLR reporter assays to mechanistically deconstruct this phenomenon is novel. This approach integrates Torchilin's expertise in advanced liposome engineering with cutting-edge (pre-2026) immunology reporter tools to gain unprecedented insight into signaling kinetics.

proposals.title: Development of a Broad-Spectrum Liposomal Vaccine Platform Co-Loaded with Multiple Lipopolysaccharide Antigens for Enhanced Immunogenicity Against Gram-Negative Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) is a key surface antigen for many Gram-negative bacteria, including pathogenic Neisseria species, but its inherent toxicity and serogroup-specific immunity limit vaccine utility. Pre-2026, monovalent LPS vaccines have shown promise but offer narrow protection. A multi-valent formulation is needed to address the diversity of clinically relevant serogroups and species, potentially preventing sepsis and meningitis caused by these pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating detoxified LPS from multiple Neisseria serogroups (e.g., A, B, C, W, Y) into a single long-circulating PEGylated liposome will create a safe, multi-valent vaccine that elicits a broader and more potent protective antibody response compared to monovalent LPS formulations or a simple mixture of monovalent liposomes.
proposals.method: We will prepare long-circulating PEGylated liposomes using established lipid film hydration/extrusion techniques. Detoxified LPS (e.g., via alkaline hydrolysis to create LOS) from selected serogroups will be incorporated into the liposomal bilayer during formulation. Liposome composition, size, and LPS incorporation efficiency will be characterized. Immunogenicity will be tested in murine models, comparing antibody titers (IgG, IgM) and opsonophagocytic activity against each serogroup for the multi-valent liposome versus controls (monovalent liposomes, free LPS mixture, alum-adjuvanted LPS).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a novel multi-antigen liposomal carrier system for bacterial vaccines, directly extending Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating liposomes. It will provide critical data on the feasibility and immunological advantage of co-presenting multiple LPS antigens on a single nanoparticle, potentially leading to a new generation of broad-spectrum anti-bacterial vaccines.
proposals.novelty: While liposomes have been used as carriers for single antigens or adjuvants, the strategic co-incorporation of multiple, distinct LPS molecules into a single liposome preparation to create a synthetic, multi-valent 'mosaic' antigen-presenting platform is a novel approach not widely reported pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Droplet Platform for High-Throughput Screening of Liposome-Encapsulated LPS Formulations for Controlled Immunogenicity
proposals.motivation: Lipopolysaccharide (LPS), a potent immunostimulant, holds promise for vaccine adjuvants and cancer immunotherapy but requires precise formulation to control its severe systemic toxicity. Pre-2026, liposome encapsulation is a leading strategy to modulate LPS activity, but screening the vast parameter space of lipid composition, surface charge, and LPS loading for optimal immunogenicity is slow and material-intensive using bulk methods. A high-throughput screening platform is urgently needed to accelerate the development of safe and effective liposomal LPS immunotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a water-in-oil droplet microfluidic system can generate monodisperse liposome libraries encapsulating LPS, enabling rapid, parallelized screening of formulation parameters against key immune cell activation readouts to identify candidates with tailored, desirable immunogenicity.
proposals.method: We will develop a droplet microfluidic chip integrating a staggered herringbone micromixer for efficient lipid hydration and a flow-focusing junction to generate picoliter aqueous droplets in a fluorinated oil continuous phase. Each droplet will act as a micro-reactor for liposome formation via solvent diffusion, with formulation variables (e.g., lipid type, PEGylation, charge) controlled by inlet streams. The resulting library will be incubated with NF-κB reporter RAW 264.7 macrophage cells arrayed in a nanowell plate. High-content imaging will quantify GFP expression (NF-κB activation) and cell viability for each formulation in a high-throughput manner.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel integrated platform for the rapid generation and immunological screening of nanocarrier libraries, directly linking formulation to biological function. It will yield a dataset correlating specific liposomal parameters with degrees of innate immune activation, providing a rational design map for LPS-based therapeutics. The platform itself will be a transferable tool for screening other encapsulated immunomodulators.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for liposome synthesis, its application to create and screen libraries of a highly toxic, clinically relevant immunostimulant like LPS is novel. The direct coupling of on-chip formulation with parallelized cell-based immunogenicity assays in a miniaturized format represents a significant advance over sequential, bulk-scale approaches.

proposals.title: Liposomal Lipopolysaccharide as a Targeted Immunostimulatory Carrier for Co-Delivery of Antigen and Adjuvant to Dendritic Cells
proposals.motivation: Effective cancer vaccines and immunotherapies require co-delivery of antigen and adjuvant to the same antigen-presenting cells to generate robust, antigen-specific T-cell responses. Pre-2026, liposomes are established drug carriers, and LPS is a potent TLR4 agonist, but its systemic toxicity limits use. This project addresses the need for a safe, targeted, and synergistic delivery platform that combines immunostimulation with precise antigen co-encapsulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that liposomes incorporating detoxified LPS (e.g., monophosphoryl lipid A, MPLA) into their bilayer will serve as a targeted, long-circulating carrier that simultaneously delivers encapsulated antigen and membrane-integrated adjuvant to dendritic cells, eliciting a superior cytotoxic T-lymphocyte response compared to separately delivered components.
proposals.method: We will prepare PEGylated liposomes using a lipid film hydration method, incorporating MPLA into the bilayer and encapsulating model antigen ovalbumin. Liposomes will be characterized for size, zeta potential, and encapsulation efficiency. In vitro, we will assess dendritic cell uptake (using flow cytometry), maturation markers (CD80, CD86, MHC-II), and antigen cross-presentation. In vivo, using a murine melanoma model (B16-OVA), we will evaluate biodistribution via fluorescent labeling, antigen-specific CD8+ T-cell induction (tetramer staining), and therapeutic antitumor efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, integrated liposomal platform for synergistic antigen-adjuvant delivery, directly building on Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating and intracellularly targeted liposomes. It will provide proof-of-concept for using liposomal LPS derivatives as both a structural component and an immunostimulant, potentially leading to more effective cancer vaccine formulations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the inverse use of LPS not as a free toxin but as an integral, detoxified structural and functional component of a liposomal carrier designed for simultaneous intracellular delivery of antigen and adjuvant, creating a unified 'immunosome'.

proposals.title: Antigen-Mediated Targeted Gene Delivery via DNA-Binding Polymer Conjugates to B-Cell Lymphoma Cells
proposals.motivation: Targeted gene delivery to specific immune cell populations, such as malignant B-cells, remains a significant challenge. Current antibody-based targeting strategies are limited by the need to conjugate cargo to the antibody itself, which can be complex and affect function. A simpler, more modular system is needed for efficient intracellular delivery of genetic material to cells defined by their surface immunoglobulin receptors.
proposals.hypothesis: Conjugating a DNA-binding cationic polymer (e.g., polyethylenimine, PEI) to a well-defined antigen (e.g., hen egg lysozyme) will create a modular delivery vehicle that selectively binds and internalizes into B-cells expressing the complementary surface antibody, enabling targeted transfection with plasmid DNA encoding a reporter or therapeutic gene.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a conjugate of PEI (25 kDa) with the model antigen hen egg lysozyme (HEL) using a heterobifunctional PEG-based crosslinker (SM(PEG)12). The conjugate will be characterized for size, charge, and DNA condensation ability. We will use a murine B-cell lymphoma line (A20) engineered to express a surface B-cell receptor (BCR) specific for HEL, with a non-specific B-cell line as a control. In vitro, we will test the transfection efficiency of the PEI-HEL/pDNA (e.g., encoding GFP) complexes via flow cytometry and confocal microscopy, comparing them to non-targeted PEI/pDNA complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for a novel 'inverted targeting' paradigm where the antigen, not the antibody, is the targeting moiety. It will provide a new, potentially simpler strategy for B-cell-specific gene delivery. The findings could open avenues for immunotherapy and genetic manipulation of antibody-expressing cells.
proposals.novelty: While antibody-targeted liposomes and polymers are well-established, using the antigen as the targeting ligand to deliver cargo to antibody-bearing cells is a conceptually inverted and largely unexplored approach. This leverages the cell's own receptor clustering and internalization machinery in a novel way.

proposals.title: In Vivo Immunogenicity and Toxicity Assessment of Long-Circulating PEGylated Liposome-Antibody-DNA Complexes for Targeted Gene Delivery
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, targeted nanocarriers like antibody-conjugated liposomes show immense promise for gene delivery, but their clinical translation is hindered by unpredictable immune reactions. Complexes containing DNA, a known immunostimulant, and PEGylated lipids, which can induce anti-PEG antibodies, present a unique and poorly understood immunogenicity risk. A systematic safety evaluation is therefore critical for advancing these multifunctional constructs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NPLL-antibody-DNA complexes will exhibit a manageable immunogenic and toxicological profile in vivo, with any immune response being primarily driven by the DNA payload and modulated by the PEGylated liposome surface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize long-circulating PEGylated liposomes (NPLLs) loaded with a model plasmid DNA and surface-conjugated with a monoclonal antibody (e.g., anti-myosin for cardiac targeting). In BALB/c mice, complexes will be administered intravenously. Immunogenicity will be assessed by measuring anti-PEG IgM/IgG, anti-DNA antibodies, and cytokine levels (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ) via ELISA at multiple time points. Toxicity will be evaluated through hematology, serum biochemistry (ALT, BUN), and histopathology of major organs (liver, spleen, kidneys). Biodistribution will be tracked using liposomes co-loaded with a near-infrared dye for optical imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the in vivo immune response to a targeted, DNA-loaded PEGylated liposomal system. It will establish critical structure-immunogenicity relationships, identifying which component (PEG, antibody, DNA) is the primary driver of any adverse reaction. The findings will guide the rational design of safer, next-generation immunoevasive nanocarriers for gene therapy.
proposals.novelty: While immunogenicity of individual components (PEG, DNA) is known, their combined effect within a single, actively targeted long-circulating liposome construct has not been systematically investigated. This proposal uniquely focuses on the conjugate as an integrated system, which is more relevant to clinical application.

proposals.title: Antibody-Targeted, Stimuli-Responsive Polymeric Micelles for Intracellular Delivery of Iodinated Blood-Pool Contrast Agents
proposals.motivation: Targeted delivery of diagnostic agents to specific cell populations remains a major challenge in molecular imaging. While antibody-conjugated carriers like immunoliposomes show promise, their efficacy is often limited by inefficient intracellular release of the payload, particularly for non-endocytosing targets or those requiring cytosolic delivery. Pre-2026 work, including Torchilin's own on TAT-liposomes and long-circulating gadolinium liposomes, highlights the need for smarter, stimuli-responsive carriers that can selectively unload their cargo inside cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering monoclonal antibody-conjugated polymeric micelles, loaded with an iodinated contrast agent, with pH-sensitive (hydrazone) or reducible (disulfide) linkers between the antibody and polymer will enable stable circulation and targeted binding, followed by efficient intracellular release of the contrast agent, enhancing specific cellular imaging.
proposals.method: We will synthesize PEG-PE-based polymeric micelles loaded with a lipophilic iodinated compound (e.g., Iohexol derivative). Monoclonal antibodies (e.g., against a specific endothelial marker) will be conjugated to the micelle surface via two distinct cleavable linkers: a pH-sensitive hydrazone bond and a reducible disulfide bond. The constructs will be characterized for size, loading, and stability in serum. In vitro studies will assess binding and internalization in target cells using flow cytometry and confocal microscopy, comparing linker strategies under acidic (pH 5.0-6.0) and reducing (presence of glutathione) conditions to quantify intracellular iodine release and contrast enhancement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct comparative analysis of two major cleavable linker strategies in the context of antibody-targeted micellar contrast agents. It will establish a proof-of-concept for an intracellularly activatable blood-pool imaging probe, moving beyond vascular compartment imaging to specific cellular visualization. The findings will inform the design of next-generation 'smart' theranostic nanocarriers.
proposals.novelty: While cleavable linkers are established in prodrug and drug delivery, their systematic integration and comparison for controlling the intracellular release of a diagnostic imaging agent from an antibody-polymer conjugate is novel. This approach merges Torchilin's expertise in long-circulating carriers and intracellular delivery (TAT) with a stimuli-responsive, target-activated imaging strategy.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Antibody-Targeted Polymeric DNA Complexes: Predicting Blood Clearance and Tissue Distribution for Enhanced Gene Delivery
proposals.motivation: Antibody-polymer-DNA complexes represent a promising strategy for targeted gene delivery, but their in vivo fate remains poorly understood. Rational design is hindered by a lack of predictive models for their pharmacokinetics (PK), particularly how antibody valency, polymer architecture, and DNA payload influence blood pool retention, RES uptake, and target tissue accumulation. This gap limits the translation of these sophisticated non-viral vectors from in vitro success to in vivo efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: The blood clearance and tissue distribution of antibody-polymer-DNA complexes can be accurately predicted using a multi-compartment pharmacokinetic model that incorporates key physicochemical parameters (size, charge, antibody affinity/valency) and known biological clearance pathways (opsonization, MPS uptake, renal filtration).
proposals.method: We will develop a physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model using data from in vivo studies in rodents. Complexes will be synthesized with varying anti-ICAM-1 antibody valency, PEGylated polymer architecture, and plasmid DNA load, then radiolabeled with I-125 for gamma-scintigraphy. Blood clearance curves and biodistribution data (heart, liver, spleen, kidneys, lungs, tumor) will be fitted to a model built on established PK principles for liposomes and proteins, using software like SAAM II. Key fitted parameters will include central compartment clearance rates, tissue distribution coefficients, and MPS uptake constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework linking the design parameters of antibody-polymer-DNA complexes to their in vivo PK profiles. It will identify the dominant factors controlling circulation time and target accumulation, offering a roadmap for optimizing complex design for specific gene therapy applications. The model will serve as a critical tool for de-risking the development of future targeted nucleic acid delivery systems.
proposals.novelty: While PK models exist for liposomes (e.g., long-circulating PEGylated types) and antibodies separately, applying and adapting this modeling framework to their complex, multi-component conjugates for gene delivery is novel. The integration of antibody binding kinetics with carrier biodistribution models represents a significant advance over empirical optimization.

ideas.title: Site-Specific, Genetically Encoded Click Chemistry for Oriented Antibody-Liposome Conjugation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the critical flaw of random antibody orientation by genetically engineering antibodies to contain a unique bioorthogonal chemical handle at a specific site in the Fc region. The engineered tag, such as a formylglycine-generating peptide sequence, allows for enzymatic installation of a reactive aldehyde group that is orthogonal to native biomolecules. This aldehyde handle then undergoes a specific, rapid, and catalyst-free click reaction (e.g., oxime or hydrazine ligation) with a complementary functional group on the liposome surface. The result is a homogeneous population of immunoliposomes with perfectly oriented antibodies, ensuring maximal target binding, improved batch consistency, and enhanced therapeutic potential.

ideas.title: Endothelium-targeted multi-compartment liposomes for dual-pathway intervention in inflammatory cancers
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel class of multi-compartment, endothelium-targeted liposomes designed for complex, inflammation-driven cancers. The platform combines a cytotoxic drug in one compartment with a novel biotherapeutic anti-inflammatory agent, such as a cytokine inhibitor or engineered protein, in a separate compartment, all guided by a vascular targeting ligand. This enables simultaneous, localized delivery to disrupt both the tumor cell proliferation and the pathological inflammatory signaling within the tumor microenvironment and vasculature. The precise co-targeting aims to enhance efficacy, reduce systemic toxicity, and overcome resistance mechanisms inherent in treating advanced metastatic or refractory cancers.

ideas.title: Closed-loop in silico-in vivo optimization of targeted liposomes using integrated PK/PD modeling
ideas.description: This project establishes a feedback-driven platform that combines computational modeling with experimental validation to design robust antibody-targeted liposomes. A mechanism-based PK/PD model, incorporating processes like the ABC phenomenon, binding site barriers, and intracellular drug release, is calibrated with initial in vitro and in vivo data. The model then guides the selection of key formulation variables (antibody density, PEG architecture, lipid composition) by simulating their impact on overall tumor drug delivery, creating a prioritized design space. Each iterative cycle of model prediction, in vivo testing, and model refinement converges on a clinically predictive optimal formulation that balances targeting, stealth, and payload delivery efficiency.

ideas.title: Dual-Stimuli AND-Gate Liposomes for Pathway-Specific Endosomal Release
ideas.description: This redesigned system engineers liposomes with a release mechanism that requires the simultaneous presence of two endosomal triggers, such as acidic pH and a specific protease. A stabilizing peptide-lipid conjugate acts as a molecular lock, where the acidic pH first induces a conformational change that exposes a protease cleavage site. Drug release occurs only upon subsequent cleavage by the endosomal protease, creating an AND-logic gate. This dual-requirement ensures highly specific activation exclusively within the intended endo-lysosomal pathway, dramatically improving reliability and reducing off-target release compared to single-stimulus designs.

ideas.title: Replace full antibody with a synthetic, minimal binding scaffold inspired by enzyme active site mimicry
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that antigen binding must be performed by an antibody or its direct fragments. Drawing an analogy from enzyme engineering, where synthetic catalysts are built by mimicking only the essential active site within a minimal scaffold, we propose to replace the whole antibody with a synthetic, minimal binding scaffold. The analogous solution involves identifying and constructing only the core functional geometry. Adapted to antibody engineering, this means designing a non-immunoglobulin protein scaffold that presents the essential binding loops (CDRs) in a stable, minimal configuration. This approach aims to drastically reduce size and immunogenicity while improving tissue penetration, moving beyond antibody-derived fragments to a fully synthetic binding molecule paradigm.

ideas.title: Half-Life Engineered Minimal Binding Scaffolds (HEMBS)
ideas.description: This approach creates a new class of therapeutic agents by fusing small, high-affinity binding domains (e.g., scFvs, nanobodies, DARPins) to a computationally designed, minimal FcRn-binding module. The FcRn module is humanized and de-immunized to prevent new immunogenicity concerns. The linker and overall architecture are optimized to balance prolonged serum half-life through FcRn-mediated recycling with enhanced penetration into dense tissues or tumors. This results in a molecule with the penetration and low immunogenicity of a fragment but the favorable pharmacokinetics of a full antibody.

ideas.title: Temporally programmed liposomes for sequentially staged combination therapy
ideas.description: This system employs multi-compartment liposomes engineered with distinct stimulus-sensitive barriers to enable controlled sequential drug release, rather than simultaneous co-delivery. The outer compartment releases an anti-inflammatory agent first, triggered by early pathological signals at the disease site to modulate the microenvironment. The inner core then releases a cytotoxic drug on a separate, later trigger, which can be a different biochemical signal or an externally applied stimulus. This temporal decoupling allows each drug to act during its optimal therapeutic window, maximizing synergy while minimizing interference and off-target effects.

ideas.title: Proximity-Triggered Covalent Locking of High-Affinity, Non-Covalent Drug-Ligand Complexes
ideas.description: This system designs targeting ligands and drug carriers with complementary, bio-orthogonal reactive groups that are inert in circulation. They are held together initially by engineered ultra-high-affinity protein-protein interactions (e.g., coiled-coils or antibody-antigen pairs) for stable delivery. Upon binding and internalization into the acidic or reductive environment of the target cell's endosome, or simply via the dramatic increase in local concentration upon target engagement, the reactive groups undergo a proximity-enabled, rapid covalent reaction. This creates a stable, irreversible link exclusively at the disease site, ensuring the drug payload remains attached to its targeting module throughout the critical delivery process, thereby maximizing therapeutic index.

ideas.title: Dual-Modality Cross-Validation of Targeted Liposome Biodistribution to Control for the Probe Effect
ideas.description: This redesigned study addresses the critical flaw of the 'probe effect' by implementing a dual-label, dual-imaging approach for in vivo tracking. Liposomes are engineered with both a near-infrared fluorophore and a chelated long-lived positron-emitting radionuclide, enabling simultaneous longitudinal quantification via FMT and PET imaging. The core innovation is the direct comparison of spatiotemporal distribution data from the two independent modalities to identify and correct for artifacts caused by label instability or altered carrier behavior. This cross-validation ensures that reported pharmacokinetics and biodistribution accurately reflect the fate of the intact therapeutic liposome, not a dissociated or modified tracking probe.

ideas.title: Computational Design of High-Affinity, Non-Covalent Peptide Adapters for Targeted Drug Delivery
ideas.description: This research direction integrates computational docking and molecular modeling to design bispecific peptide ligands. These peptides are engineered with one domain that non-covalently binds a specific cell surface target (like CD163) by mimicking an antibody paratope, and a separate domain that forms a high-affinity, non-covalent complex with a therapeutic payload (e.g., a small molecule or protein drug). The synergy enables the rapid in silico screening and optimization of adapter-drug pairs for optimal binding kinetics and specificity, creating a versatile platform where drugs and targeting modules can be mixed and matched without covalent conjugation chemistry, facilitating personalized and adaptable therapeutic regimens.

ideas.title: Logic-Gated Precision Depletion of Pathogenic Macrophage Subsets in Autoimmune Disease
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond broad macrophage depletion to a precision strategy. A targeted delivery platform uses disease-specific surface markers to localize to sites of inflammation, where a logic-gated payload release mechanism is triggered only by the local pathological microenvironment. This dual-selectivity system aims to eliminate the specific pro-inflammatory macrophage subsets responsible for tissue damage while sparing protective, homeostatic macrophages and other immune cells. The goal is to achieve potent anti-inflammatory effects without causing systemic immunosuppression or disrupting normal tissue repair functions.

ideas.title: Use Endothelial 'Bait' to Capture and Modulate Circulating Cells
ideas.description: This idea inverts the therapeutic target from the vessel wall to the blood cells by applying a solution from fishery science. The analogous problem is bycatch mitigation, where specific lures or exclusion devices are used to selectively interact with target species based on their innate behaviors. The adapted solution is to create therapeutic 'endothelial bait'—nanoparticles or soluble multimeric ligands that mimic the endothelial surface. When injected into the bloodstream, this bait exploits the natural homing instinct of specific circulating cells, binding them and sequestering them from the vessel wall. This could prevent pathological inflammation or metastasis by intercepting cells in circulation, offering a dynamic, systemic alternative to static endothelial targeting.

ideas.title: Engineered Circulating Cell Anchors Using Stable Endothelial Epitopes
ideas.description: This approach redesigns circulating blood cells or synthetic carriers to display high-affinity, synthetic ligands targeting constitutively expressed and stable endothelial surface proteins, rather than relying on natural adhesion molecules. The engineered ligands are selected for shear-independent binding to overcome the transient nature of physiological rolling interactions. This creates a stable platform for delivering therapeutics or diagnostic agents directly to the vascular lumen surface from the bloodstream, enabling localized action without requiring extravasation.

ideas.title: Adapting Radio-Tracer Kinetic Modeling to Quantify Targeted Uptake in Macrophage Subtypes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of a parallel problem in nuclear medicine/pharmacokinetics, where radio-tracer uptake in different tissue compartments is quantified using compartmental kinetic modeling. The analogous solution involves using dynamic data and mathematical models to derive quantitative parameters like influx rate constants, separating targeted delivery from non-specific background. Adapted to the research context, this approach would involve administering a CD163-targeted agent (e.g., antibody-drug conjugate or nanoparticle) and using techniques like intravital microscopy or serial flow cytometry on explanted tissues to collect time-course uptake data for M1 macrophages, M2 macrophages, and other CD163-expressing monocytes within a complex tissue microenvironment. This dynamic data would then be fitted to a purpose-built kinetic model to calculate subtype-specific rate constants for binding (k_on) and internalization (k_int), providing a rigorous, quantitative comparison of targeting efficacy that accounts for differences in vascular access, receptor density, and non-specific phagocytic activity.

ideas.title: Dual-Function Nanocarrier for Sequential Payload Delivery
ideas.description: This idea expands the original 'dual problem' concept by drawing an analogy from precision agriculture, where smart delivery systems enable sequential, condition-dependent release of multiple agents. The analogous solution uses core-shell nanostructures that release a protective payload first, followed by a growth-promoting one, triggered by environmental cues. Adapted to oncology, we propose a dual-function nanocarrier that uses the same targeting system (e.g., to the CD206 receptor on tumor-associated macrophages) but incorporates a sequential release mechanism. The nanoparticle's outer shell contains an agent to inhibit key immunosuppressive signals (e.g., blocking CSF-1R or TGF-β), which is shed upon encountering the tumor microenvironment. This initial release 'primes' the macrophages and the local niche. Subsequently, the exposed core degrades to deliver the immunostimulatory drug (e.g., a CD40 agonist or STING agonist), thereby more effectively repolarizing the macrophages to an anti-tumor state. This approach aims to overcome the limitation of delivering a single repolarizing agent into a dominantly immunosuppressive context.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Macrophage Reprogramming via Spatially-Controlled Nanocarrier Delivery
ideas.description: This research direction develops a platform of ultra-small, multifunctional nanocarriers capable of targeting and delivering payloads to individual macrophages based on their specific surface phenotype. The core innovation is the integration of a sensing and feedback mechanism: the nanocarriers not only deliver immunostimulatory agents to repolarize pro-tumor macrophages but also carry reporters that signal successful intracellular delivery and initial phenotypic shift. This allows for real-time, single-cell resolution monitoring of the immunotherapeutic intervention within the heterogeneous tumor mass. Consequently, the system moves beyond static delivery to enable adaptive, spatially-precise reprogramming of the tumor immune landscape, potentially overcoming microenvironmental resistance mechanisms.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune-System-Inspired Targeting for Pro-Inflammatory Macrophage Depletion
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of adaptive threat defense from cybersecurity to the treatment of systemic autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis. The analogous cybersecurity solution uses continuous monitoring and dynamic rule updates to target only active, evolving threats. Adapted here, the research proposes a therapeutic platform that does not broadly deplete all pro-inflammatory macrophages. Instead, it integrates real-time biosensors to monitor the disease microenvironment, identifies a dynamic 'threat signature' of the most pathogenic macrophage subsets, and uses this data to guide the precise, on-demand depletion of only those targeted cells. This approach aims to improve efficacy by focusing on the key drivers of inflammation while potentially reducing off-target effects and preserving beneficial immune functions.

ideas.title: Contextually Activated Reprogramming of Tumor-Associated Macrophages
ideas.description: This approach moves from indiscriminate depletion to conditional functional reprogramming of macrophages within the tumor microenvironment. The system delivers a therapeutic payload designed to disrupt specific pro-tumor signaling pathways in macrophages, but its activity is locked until activated by a tumor-specific trigger, such as an overexpressed protease or the acidic pH of the TME. This spatial restriction aims to convert tumor-supportive M2-like macrophages toward an anti-tumor phenotype while sparing their essential physiological functions in normal tissues. The goal is to disrupt the tumor's support network without causing the systemic toxicity and impaired healing associated with broad macrophage depletion.

ideas.title: Adapting Supramolecular Host-Guest Chemistry for Non-Covalent Drug-Targeting Ligand Assembly
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that drugs must be directly and covalently conjugated to their targeting ligands. By drawing an analogy from supramolecular chemistry, which has solved the problem of creating stable, specific assemblies without covalent bonds through engineered host-guest pairs (e.g., cucurbiturils, cyclodextrins), we propose adapting this solution. The adapted research idea is to develop a modular drug delivery platform based on a synthetic, high-affinity supramolecular 'lock-and-key' pair. One member of the pair (e.g., a synthetic macrocyclic host) would be functionalized onto the drug or a nanocarrier, while the complementary guest molecule would be attached to the targeting ligand (e.g., an antibody fragment, peptide, or aptamer). This system would enable the spontaneous, non-covalent, and high-affinity assembly of drug-targeting complexes in situ, offering advantages in modularity, simplified manufacturing, and potential for triggered release.

ideas.title: Adaptive endothelial targeting via closed-loop sensing and dynamic therapeutic adjustment
ideas.description: This project moves beyond static multi-targeting by creating an adaptive system that continuously senses the tumor endothelial target landscape *in vivo*. A sensing module, employing intravital imaging or analysis of circulating endothelial signatures, provides real-time feedback on determinant expression patterns and their changes under therapeutic pressure. This data drives an algorithmic controller that adjusts the formulation of a multi-agent therapeutic cocktail (e.g., bispecific antibodies, aptamers) in a programmable infusion system. The result is a dynamically personalized intervention that preemptively overcomes adaptive resistance and spatial heterogeneity by evolving its targeting strategy in sync with the disease.

ideas.title: Closed-loop computational-experimental platform for iterative design and validation of CD163 peptide ligands
ideas.description: This adversarially improved approach establishes an iterative cycle of computational design and high-throughput experimental validation to overcome the poor predictive accuracy of standalone docking. Peptides designed in silico targeting the CD163 antibody site are synthesized on an array and screened for binding affinity and specificity using label-free biosensors. The resulting experimental data is fed back to retrain and parameterize the molecular modeling and scoring functions, creating a self-improving design system. This closed loop ensures that each design round is informed by real-world binding outcomes, dramatically increasing the robustness and success rate of generating viable peptide ligands.

ideas.title: A Human-Relevant Translation Pipeline for Endothelial Interventions
ideas.description: This redesigned approach establishes a systematic pipeline to de-risk the translation of endothelial-targeting interventions from animals to humans. It mandates a rigorous intermediate phase using human cell-based microphysiological systems (organ-chips), patient-derived endothelial cells, and quantitative systems pharmacology models to validate mechanisms and predict human-specific dosing and safety profiles. This human-relevant data is then used to design more precise and informed first-in-human clinical trials, significantly increasing the likelihood of successful validation and reducing trial failure due to lack of efficacy or unexpected toxicity.

ideas.title: Adapting Precision Agriculture Sensing to Map and Target Diverse Pathological Endothelia
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from precision agriculture, where remote sensing and variable-rate application solve the problem of heterogeneous field conditions. The analogous problem in vascular biology is the heterogeneity of endothelial pathological phenotypes across diseases and vascular beds, which reduces the efficiency of uniform targeting strategies. The adapted research idea is to develop a diagnostic-therapeutic platform that first uses advanced multi-parametric imaging (analogous to agricultural sensing) to non-invasively map the specific molecular, functional, and metabolic signatures of diseased endothelium in different contexts. This 'pathophenotype map' then informs and controls the delivery of targeted agents, using smart carriers designed to respond to the unique combination of signals in each pathological microenvironment. This approach aims to move beyond a one-target-fits-all model to a precision targeting system that dynamically adapts to local endothelial disease states, dramatically improving therapeutic efficiency and specificity.

ideas.title: Applying Satellite Constellation Optimization to Drug Carrier Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from aerospace engineering for optimizing satellite constellations to the problem of optimizing drug carrier design. In satellite design, engineers systematically model parameters like altitude, orbital inclination, and satellite count to achieve optimal coverage and robustness. Analogously, we propose a computational framework to treat ligand-decorated drug carriers as a targeting constellation. The research would involve developing and validating a multi-objective optimization model that simulates how the interdependent parameters of carrier size (influencing margination and diffusion), ligand density (affecting multivalent binding), and conjugation chemistry (determining ligand orientation and stability) collectively govern the dynamic processes of endothelial anchoring and subsequent internalization, thereby identifying Pareto-optimal design spaces for specific vascular targets.

ideas.title: Model CD163 as a dynamically tunable receptor using control theory from electrical engineering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that CD163 expression is static by drawing an analogy from electrical engineering control systems. In that field, components have dynamic, tunable properties modeled via transfer functions and system identification. The analogous solution involves characterizing how a component's behavior changes in response to input signals over time. Adapted to immunology, the research would treat the CD163 receptor not as a fixed marker but as a dynamically regulated system. The investigation would apply control theory principles: design experiments where inflammatory signals (e.g., IL-6, IL-10, LPS) are introduced as timed 'input pulses,' and measure CD163 expression/shedding as the 'output response.' Using this time-series data, a mathematical model would be built to describe the regulatory transfer function, identifying kinetic parameters, feedback delays, and saturation points. This model would then predict how inflammatory contexts (like chronic vs. acute phases) dynamically affect CD163-targeting efficacy for therapies like antibody-drug conjugates or CAR macrophages, enabling temporally optimized intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Guided Synergistic Pairing via Endothelial-Targeted Codelivery
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply attaching new drugs to an endothelial targeting moiety. It first employs single-cell omics and spatial transcriptomics of patient endothelial cells to map disease-specific dysregulated pathways. A computational model then identifies optimal, synergistic therapeutic pairs where one agent directly corrects the core endothelial defect, and a second agent exploits the normalized vasculature for enhanced efficacy. The endothelial targeting platform is then used to co-deliver this rationally selected pair, ensuring both agents act on the same cellular compartment to achieve a multiplicative therapeutic effect validated by in vivo systems pharmacology models.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Hemodynamic Platform to Optimize Drug Carrier Design for Dynamic Vascular Targeting
ideas.description: This research overcomes the static in vitro limitation by developing an integrated platform that accounts for the dynamic hemodynamic environment critical for vascular targeting. It employs a tiered system starting with high-throughput screening in tunable microfluidic vessels that replicate human endothelial physiology and wall shear stress. The data from these dynamic in vitro assays feeds a computational model that predicts carrier performance across a simulated vascular network, capturing non-linear parameter interactions. The final, model-optimized carrier designs are then validated through direct, real-time visualization of anchoring and internalization in the live circulation of transgenic zebrafish, ensuring translational relevance.

ideas.title: Single-cell mapping of nanomaterial uptake to the macrophage activation continuum in tissue
ideas.description: This approach quantifies targeting efficacy by directly measuring nanoparticle uptake within individual myeloid cells in ex vivo or in vivo complex tissues using high-parameter single-cell analysis. A broad panel of phenotypic markers captures the full spectrum of macrophage and monocyte states without relying on outdated M1/M2 binaries. Advanced computational clustering then correlates the magnitude of uptake with specific, data-driven cellular subsets, revealing which precise phenotypic profiles, rather than presumed subtypes, are most associated with particle internalization. This yields a robust, high-resolution map of targeting efficiency across the true heterogeneity of tissue macrophages.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Dot Cellular Addressing for Single-Macrophage Nanocarrier Delivery
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of ultra-small nanocarriers for single-macrophage targeting by drawing an analogy from quantum computing and nanoscale electronics. In quantum dot cellular automata, computation is achieved through the precise, field-mediated interaction of neighboring quantum-dot cells without direct wiring. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing nanocarriers that use a similar principle of localized activation. The adapted research idea involves developing nanocarriers with a cloaked targeting system that is only unmasked or activated by a highly focused external field applied to a specific microscopic location within the heterogeneous tumor. This ensures that the carrier's precision targeting is engaged exclusively in the immediate vicinity of the field application, allowing it to bind to and deliver its payload to a single, pre-identified macrophage cell, thereby achieving true single-cell targeting precision within a dense cellular environment.

ideas.title: Applying Lineage-Specific Marker Reclassification from Developmental Biology to Challenge CD163 Exclusivity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that CD163 is an exclusive marker of the monocytic lineage by adapting a solution from developmental biology. In stem cell research, the reclassification of lineage-specific markers (e.g., Nestin) was achieved by demonstrating their context-dependent expression in other cell types during stress or specific microenvironments. We adapt this by proposing a systematic investigation to identify if other cell types (e.g., endothelial cells, epithelial cells, specific tumor cells) express CD163 under defined conditions such as chronic inflammation, tissue fibrosis, or cancer. The research would employ lineage-tracing animal models, deep single-cell profiling of human disease biopsies, and in vitro stimulation assays to map non-canonical CD163 expression. The goal is to determine if this ectopic expression is functionally relevant and could lead to off-target effects in diagnostic or therapeutic strategies targeting CD163.
